title: LumenForge Systems + Redwood strategic alliance technical discussion
preview:
Meeting Header
Date: 2026-03-20
Start: 16:00 UTC
Duration: ~58 minutes
Location: Zoom
Recording ID: ff-record-20260320-lum-01
Links shared: https://redwood-internal.s3/partner-decks/lumenforge-kickoff.pdf (deck), https://tiny.redwood/lf-a100-benchmarks (benchmark spreadsheet - placeholder)

Summary:
High-level partner discovery between Redwood and LumenForge Systems to explore reseller/channel partnership and technical integration for managed GPU/cloud offerings. Discussed target customers, joint benchmarks, VPC/private deployments, KMS integration, and procurement/timeline expectations. Agreed on pilot scope and immediate actions to run bench on LumenForge A100 cluster.

Topics: partner program, benchmarks, private deployment (VPC), security/KMS, pricing and go-to-market motions, SI enablement

Transcript:
[00:00] Alex Chen: Hey everyone, thanks for joining, um, we really appreciate the time. I think this is the kickoff between Redwood and LumenForge to talk about potential alliance and technical fit. I see Maya, Jon, Sara, Tom, hey team.
[00:15] Maya Patel: Hi Alex, thanks, and thanks Priya and Diego. Quick context — LumenForge has a catalog of GPU-enabled cloud zones that we sell to telco and edge customers and we're looking to add an inference partner to resell and embed.
[00:29] Jon Rivera: Yeah, on the infra side we operate mixed clusters, we have A100 and H100 pools across three regions, plus some on-prem colos for key customers.
[00:42] Priya Natarajan: Cool, thanks. Can you say a bit about typical inference workloads? Latency critical, batch, seq lengths, that kind of stuff.
[00:53] Sara O'Neill: Most customers are latency-sensitive — chat/assistants and document search. Seq lengths usually 256 to 1024 tokens, some embeddings at 1024-2048 for long-form retrieval. We also run batch reranking jobs overnight.
[01:10] Diego Martinez: Got it. From Redwood side we typically propose model variants and quantization profiles depending on customer SLOs. For low-latency chat we recommend smaller quantized variants; for quality-sensitive workloads we lean toward fp16 on A100/H100.
[01:26] Tom Wu: On procurement side, we'll need reseller pricing and standard terms. Also SOC2 and some compliance docs up front.
[01:34] Alex Chen: We'll share the partner program and SOC2 pack. Quick qual questions: who's the internal champion for pilots? What's your timeline?
[01:44] Maya Patel: I'm owning GTM for partnerships; timeline is Q2 pilot ideally, proof of concept with a joint customer in mid-April if possible.
[01:53] Jon Rivera: Technical champions will be Sara and our platform team — they'll run benchmarks and host the cluster for initial testing.
[02:02] Priya Natarajan: Great. Can you describe network topology for the private deployments? Are customers in a VPC peered to your environment or do you offer on-prem installs?
[02:15] Sara O'Neill: We offer both. For cloud zones, it's VPC peering or private link into our region. For some regulated customers we do on-prem appliance installs — usually air-gapped or behind their firewall.
[02:27] Diego Martinez: That's encouraging. For private deployment we need a way to handle the control plane (redwood control plane) and customer-managed data plane. We support a private control plane model; curious how LumenForge would prefer to route traffic and manage KMS keys.
[02:40] Maya Patel: Preference is: we host the data plane and customers keep KMS in their tenancy. For some enterprise customers we'll help with key rotation via our managed KMS layer.
[02:51] Alex Chen: Ok so KMS integration and audit logs will be key items. We should surface those in the security checklist.
[02:57] Tom Wu: Also, encryption in transit and at rest — any specific requirements from Redwood that'll help us speed procurement?
[03:06] Priya Natarajan: We can provide our encryption and key lifecycle docs. Our typical asks are TLS 1.2+, disk encryption with CMKs, and audit log retention.
[03:17] Jon Rivera: Good. On capacity: how does Redwood recommend routing between hosted and dedicated instances? We want to know how to position product tiers.
[03:28] Diego Martinez: We usually advise a three-tier approach: hosted API for low-friction dev/test, dedicated reserved capacity for steady production SLOs, and private/VPC for regulated workloads. Smart routing can fall back to hosted variants when dedicated is saturated.
[03:42] Sara O'Neill: When you say fallback, is that transparent to the app, like same API and model compatibility?
[03:49] Priya Natarajan: Yes, same API and model catalog semantics. We do mapping between model aliases and underlying variant compatibilities. There can be small differences in latency/cost that we track in the console.
[04:05] Maya Patel: Sounds good. On the business side, how do you typically split reseller margins or revenue share with partners?
[04:13] Alex Chen: We have multiple models — resale margin for managed capacity, referral fees for hosted leads, and a hybrid for dedicated where partners can markup compute and support. We'll send a term sheet.
[04:25] Tom Wu: Please include sample pricing for an A100 reserved pool and an H100 option. That helps procurement.
[04:33] Alex Chen: Will do.
[04:36] [04:36] — short pause / background noise —
[04:40] Priya Natarajan: On benchmarks: we want to run a set of representative workloads — chat (64–256 tokens), long-form completions (1k tokens), embeddings at 1024, and a rerank batch job. We can produce a script and run it against your A100 cluster.
[04:56] Jon Rivera: Our ops team can provision a test slice on the A100 cluster and open an account for Redwood to run the scripts. Rough estimate: we can allocate a 4-GPU slice for 48 hours.
[05:07] Diego Martinez: That's perfect. We'll aim to run profiling and GPU utilization, p99 latency, cost per token, and throughput. We'll share a spreadsheet with results.
[05:17] Sara O'Neill: Are you bringing your own model images or do you expect to use our marketplace images?
[05:24] Priya Natarajan: We can bring our own container images; typically we provide a verified image for the model and runtime. For marketplace images we'll evaluate compatibility. There's sometimes differences in kernel optimizations.
[05:37] Jon Rivera: Good to know. We run a mix of distro kernels; our infra team will need the runtime requirements (CUDA, cuDNN, NCCL, etc.).
[05:46] Diego Martinez: We'll send a one-pager of runtime dependencies.
[05:50] Alex Chen: Let's talk risk and constraints. Any regulatory or data residency constraints we should know about?
[05:56] Maya Patel: A subset of customers require EU-only data residency and separate tenancy. For telco customers we often need strict jurisdiction controls and logging retention for seven years in some verticals.
[06:09] Priya Natarajan: Ok, that likely maps to private deployments or dedicated within EU regions with KMS in-region. We can support that with our private control plane offering; need to confirm with legal for some long-retention requirements.
[06:23] Tom Wu: On legal, do you have a standard MSA with data processing addendum? We need to align with procurement.
[06:30] Alex Chen: Yes, we have a standard MSA and DPA; we'll include that version in the partner package.
[06:35] Maya Patel: Awesome. A technical nit: how do you handle model version pinning and rollouts? We have customers that require canary and quick rollback.
[06:45] Diego Martinez: Redwood has a rollout system — canary, A/B, and automatic fallback policies. You can pin model versions per-customer and push rollouts gradually. We also support prompt/eval-based regression alerts.
[06:58] Sara O'Neill: Do you expose token-level cost breakdowns to partners or only to end customers?
[07:05] Priya Natarajan: Partners get a partner console view with aggregated metrics and cost breakdown per route/model. We can enable or restrict per-customer views.
[07:15] Jon Rivera: Ok. Any thoughts on SI enablement — training, certification? We want to enable our channel SIs.
[07:24] Alex Chen: We have partner enablement — technical bootcamps, certification for SEs, and a partner portal with docs and sample deployments. We'd love to co-build joint sales plays for telco/edge verticals.
[07:38] Maya Patel: That's a go. On pilot customers, we have a telco customer in APAC who needs low-latency inference in their edge POPs; could be a good pilot.
[07:50] Priya Natarajan: Great — we'll scope the pilot: latency targets, throughput, security controls, and success criteria.
[07:58] Tom Wu: On procurement timeline — if we agree to terms quickly we can issue a PO by early May. Any blockers?
[08:06] Alex Chen: Terms and SOC2 plus the partner discount will be the main inputs. We'll aim to provide the package this week.
[08:13] [08:13] — brief cross talk about calendar scheduling —
[08:18] Diego Martinez: A quick technical question on caching: some of our customers rely heavily on prefix caching and KV caches to scale. Does Redwood support prefix/KV cache that can be shared across requests while preserving tenant isolation?
[08:30] Priya Natarajan: Yes — we support prefix and KV caching patterns. For private deployments we can configure cache routing so caches remain tenant-specific; for shared hosted pools we have secure namespace isolation. There are tradeoffs for cache coherency and cost.
[08:46] Sara O'Neill: For multi-tenant customers, could caching inadvertently surface cross-tenant data?
[08:52] Priya Natarajan: No, we implement strict namespace separation. For some advanced caching we recommend dedicated cache instances. We'll include guidance.
[09:02] Jon Rivera: On observability — what traces/metrics do you emit and can they be forwarded to our observability stack?
[09:09] Diego Martinez: We emit per-request latency, token counts, model id, p50/p95/p99, GPU utilization and errors. We can forward traces/logs to your datadog/stack via secure endpoints.
[09:22] Maya Patel: Ok. We also need to see typical SLA numbers for dedicated capacity.
[09:28] Alex Chen: Our Dedicated SLA targets are 99.9% uptime for compute and latency SLOs that vary by instance type; we'll share a performance profile doc.
[09:37] [09:37] — someone drops for 2s, reconnects —
[09:40] Tom Wu: Quick question — taxes and export controls; do you restrict model exports to certain regions?
[09:48] Alex Chen: We follow export control laws and some models could be restricted per geography. We'll note that in the partner terms.
[09:55] Priya Natarajan: Also worth noting kernel/compilation selection: our runtime auto-selects kernels based on model shape and hardware, so perf can vary with sequence length; it's why we want to run a representative suite of tests on LumenForge infra.
[10:11] Jon Rivera: Ok let's lock down the benchmark timeframe. We can provision the slice next Tuesday for 48 hours. Does that work?
[10:19] Priya Natarajan: That works from our side. We'll send the scripts by Friday.
[10:24] Maya Patel: Good. On marketing/GTM, we want to coordinate a co-marketing one-pager if pilot is successful.
[10:31] Alex Chen: Definitely — joint case study + co-branded deck once pilot results are favorable.
[10:37] Sara O'Neill: A couple of technical edge cases: our edge nodes sometimes have limited nvlink topology — will that materially impact model throughput?
[10:46] Diego Martinez: It can; nvlink helps large model sharding and KV cache sharing. For smaller single-GPU models it's less impactful. We'll capture those requirements in the bench notes.
[10:57] Jon Rivera: We'll include topology info in the provisioning ticket.
[11:02] Priya Natarajan: Ok, I want to call out a potential POC flow: 1) Agree on pilot scope + commercial terms, 2) Redwood runs benchmarks on LumenForge test slice, 3) joint pilot with selected customer for 4–8 weeks, 4) finalize go-to-market and reseller motion.
[11:20] Maya Patel: That matches our internal process. We're comfortable with that flow.
[11:26] Alex Chen: Good. Anything else on technical blockers?
[11:30] Sara O'Neill: One more — access control/SSO. We use SAML and IdP for tenant access. Do you support SAML SSO for partner consoles?
[11:39] Diego Martinez: Yes, we support SAML, OIDC and SCIM for user provisioning. We'll deliver the SSO setup checklist.
[11:46] Tom Wu: From procurement, please include sample invoices and any reseller PO instructions.
[11:52] Alex Chen: We'll include that.
[11:54] [11:54] — attendees do short round of final clarifications —
[11:58] Maya Patel: Recap — Maya will share SI list, LumenForge will provision the A100 slice next Tue, Redwood sends benchmark scripts and partner package this week, and we reconvene after bench results.
[12:12] Alex Chen: Perfect. Thanks everyone. We'll follow up with the deck and action items.

End of meeting.

title: Distributed KV watermarking & eviction incident review - Peregrine Systems
preview: Cross-team technical review after an outage where aggressive watermarking + disk spillback caused cascading latency. Discussed root causes (watermark thresholds, noisy prefixes), mitigations (per-prefix quotas, async snapshotting), and pilot plan for hybrid eviction policy and improved telemetry.
text: Cross-team technical review after an outage where aggressive watermarking + disk spillback caused cascading latency. Discussed root causes (watermark thresholds, noisy prefixes), mitigations (per-prefix quotas, async snapshotting), and pilot plan for hybrid eviction policy and improved telemetry.

title: On-prem CUDA memory leak & retry-policy investigation - Novum Health POC
preview: Deep-dive into recurring OOMs and intermittent CUDA timeouts on Novum's on-prem POC cluster. Discussed likely memory leak patterns in KV cache + batching interaction, retry semantics, driver/version mismatches, and mitigating configs (batch caps, quantization profiles, periodic cache eviction). Agreed next steps: collect full nvidia-smi/dmesg traces, reproduce with a controlled workload, run memory-heavy stress test with debug build, and try 2 kernel/driver combinations. Priority: reduce production OOM risk and define safe retry/backoff policy.

title: Compliance deep-dive: audit telemetry & admin exports - Orion Capital
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-12-18
Start: 15:30 PST
Duration: ~48 minutes
Attendees: Alex Carter (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Samir Patel (Orion VP Eng), Lena Torres (Orion Head Sec), Derek Wu (Orion DevOps), Marta Alvarez (Orion Compliance)

Summary: quick deep-dive on what Redwood captures in audit logs, options to stream/export admin activity for evidence collection, and how customer-managed keys (CMEK) and RBAC/SSO combine for compliance. Lots of detail on field-level exports and retention tradeoffs; agreed next steps and POC.

[00:00] Alex C: hey everyone, thanks for joining, um this is Alex from Redwood, i'll hand off to Priya for the technical walk through but quick agenda: capture fields, export destinations, retention/archival, KMS/CMEK, and packaging for SOC2 evidence.
[00:16] Samir: thanks, Alex. For background, where we're coming from: we run sensitive financial models, we need admin event retention for audits — and have a Splunk instance where we want to push logs. We're also evaluating dedicated vs hosted because of data residency concerns.
[00:34] Priya: got it. quick call out — i'm going to use some shorthand. Redwood's audit/events pipeline captures console actions and API-level events. examples: API key lifecycle (create, revoke, rotate), user/admin logins, SSO assertions, RBAC role changes, model version pinning, rollout changes, route updates, and system-generated alerts. Also low-level operational events like instance reboots, scaling, and KV cache flushes — though those are less frequent in audit exports.
[00:58] Lena: do you capture failed login attempts with IP and geolocation? that's a must for our compliance audit.
[01:06] Priya: yes — failed and successful auth attempts, IP, timestamp, user-agent, and session id. geolocation is best-effort based on IP; it's included as an optional field. note: occasionally geo lookups will be blank if IP is private or obfuscated.
[01:26] Derek: what about API request/response payloads — do you log any PII or model inputs?
[01:33] Priya: good question. by default we do NOT capture full user prompt payloads in audit logs. audit events include metadata (request_id, model, route, token cost, user_id hashes) but payloads are redacted. customers can opt into richer tracing via sensitive telemetry toggles for scoped POCs, but that's not default for hosted. for private/on-prem we can enable more detailed captures if you control the environment.
[01:58] Samir: ok that aligns. can you walk through export targets? we want S3 first, then syslog to Splunk.
[02:07] Priya: export options: - daily/continuous S3 export (ndjson or gzipped JSON lines), - syslog/rsyslog over TLS, - webhook streaming (delivery to an endpoint), - Kinesis / PubSub style streaming to customer topics, - direct Splunk HEC via token. We also have an SFTP archival option for legal teams. retention/archival policies are configurable per-account and per-log-type.
[02:38] Marta: about encryption — do exports support CMEK? we need customer-managed keys with rotation and HSM-backed keys.
[02:46] Priya: yes, CMEK supported for S3 exports and snapshot backups. we integrate with major KMS providers (AWS KMS, GCP KMS, Azure KeyVault) and support HSM-managed keys where available. rotation: Redwood supports key rotation schedules, and we provide guidance for re-wrapping existing objects; note some archival operations may require short maintenance windows for re-encryption.
[03:12] Lena: do you provide proof of key usage for auditors? like key IDs and envelope metadata?
[03:18] Priya: we include key-id metadata in export manifests and receipts. for deeper HSM attestation you'll need vendor-specific certs — we can bundle a manifest showing key-id, creation date, and rotation events as part of the evidence pack.
[03:33] Alex C: quick practical note — for hosted customers we manage the control plane logs and provide the audit exports; for Dedicated/Private customers you can opt to keep logs entirely on your VPC. this matters for residency.
[03:48] Samir: we are definitely interested in keeping logs in our EU region. is regional export available?
[03:53] Priya: yes — exports can be region-locked. if you want S3 in eu-west-1 we can push to that endpoint and ensure the control plane mounts the export pipeline in eu-west. for staging POC we usually recommend eu-west staging to validate.
[04:10] Derek: about retention windows — we're thinking 90 days for admins, 365 for security incidents. can you set per-event-type retention?
[04:18] Priya: yes, retention is per-log-type: admin_activity, auth_events, system_events, model_serving_telemetry. you can set different retention buckets and automatic archival to cold storage after X days. cost will vary.
[04:30] Samir: how do you support search/queries for audit evidence? we need to be able to pull an event timeline for an incident.
[04:37] Priya: we provide two patterns: 1) push to your SIEM (Splunk/Datadog) for native search, or 2) use Redwood Console 'Evidence export' where you can select timeframe + entity and download a signed gzipped bundle with manifest and hashes for tamper-evidence. the console export is good for ad-hoc auditor requests.
[05:03] Lena: signed bundle — what's the signing algorithm and tamper proofing?
[05:08] Priya: we sign manifests with an internal RSA 2048 key and include sha256 hashes for each file; for Dedicated/Private customers we can accept your PGP key to sign bundles with your key. note: the internal signature is just to show chain-of-custody; auditors sometimes ask for customer-signed artifacts which we can support for POC.
[05:28] Samir: any SLA on export delivery? i.e., guarantee that events will be available in S3 within X minutes?
[05:33] Alex C: we have export latency SLAs for Dedicated customers; hosted customers get best-effort (typical <30s for streaming, <5 minutes for batch S3 writes). for strict compliance you'd pick Dedicated with guaranteed export latency and replication.
[05:53] Derek: can we redact or mask fields before export? e.g., mask last 4 digits of account numbers?
[05:58] Priya: yes — we have field masking rules you can configure. masking occurs prior to export and is applied at the pipeline level. for complex transformations we offer a pre-export lambda hook for Dedicated/private where you can run custom logic.
[06:18] Marta: SOC2 evidence — what do you provide out of the box?
[06:22] Alex C: standard pack includes SOC2 Type II summary, control mappings, audit log manifests, RBAC change history for the period, SSO/SAML config logs, and KMS rotation records. we'll send a redacted sample pack after this call.
[06:37] Lena: we need to show user provisioning via SCIM — does Redwood log SCIM requests and responses?
[06:43] Priya: SCIM events for provisioning/deprovisioning are logged with operation, actor, timestamp, and target user details (no password). those are included in the admin_activity feed.
[06:55] Samir: what about retention of deleted logs — can logs be purged earlier? we're trying to balance privacy requests.
[07:02] Priya: we support legal holds for specific entities to keep logs beyond retention. we also support GDPR right-to-be-forgotten workflows: you can request redaction of user-identifying fields; redaction will be applied to future logs but deletion of past artifacts is handled case-by-case and may require legal review.
[07:22] Derek: integration with our SIEM — field maps and sample data please.
[07:27] Priya: will share sample mapping file (json schema) and a 48-hour sample export. that's on the action items.
[07:33] Samir: quick question — do you surface admin actions taken by Redwood support engineers?
[07:39] Priya: yes, support/admin access is audited. actions by Redwood staff are flagged and a justification/approval chain is logged; for Dedicated customers you can disable remote support or require explicit approval flows.
[07:56] Alex C: small note — sometimes transcripts show product names wrong in our docs, like 'K-V cash' instead of 'KV cache' in older exports, just FYI, we fixed that in the console text but legacy exports may have transcribe noise.
[08:12] Lena: on the signing — is the manifest enough for auditors or do they ask for more?
[08:18] Priya: auditors often want chain-of-custody: manifest, storage receipts, KMS key activity, IAM change logs. we can assemble all that into a single evidence bundle.
[08:30] Marta: what's the typical cost overhead for 365-day retention vs 90-day?
[08:36] Alex C: ballpark: 365-day hot storage is roughly 2.5x cost vs 90-day hot; cold archiving after 90 days reduces cost significantly. we'll include pricing tiers in the follow-up email.
[08:48] Derek: if we choose CMEK with rotation, is there any downtime risk on rewrap?
[08:53] Priya: minimal; we schedule rewrap jobs during low-traffic windows; in some big re-encrypt operations we recommend a short maintenance window for consistency checks. we'll share runbook.
[09:06] Samir: ok actions look good. any blockers for POC?
[09:10] Alex C: we'd need an S3 staging bucket with a KMS key you own (for CMEK) and Splunk HEC token if you want live ingestion; Priya will own the config on our side.
[09:20] Priya: i'll draft the export config and share the sample.
[09:24] Lena: final question — do you track admin changes to RBAC at the fine-grain level, like permission grants and role-to-user assignments?
[09:30] Priya: yes, role changes, permission deltas, and the actor (user/service) are included; we also store previous vs new state in the change event so you can reconstruct.
[09:42] Samir: great. we might request a short custom field (our internal account_id) be included — is that possible?
[09:48] Priya: yes, we support mapping custom customer fields into the audit events at ingestion time, or you can enrich downstream in Splunk.
[09:55] Alex C: perfect. let's recap next steps — Priya ship sample schema and 2-day gz export, Alex send SOC2/ISO docs, schedule POC; customer to confirm retention windows and provide Splunk endpoint.
[10:12] Lena: sounds good. thanks everyone.
[10:16] Samir: thanks, bye.

Notes about transcript quality: automated transcription with minor speaker label swaps and typical ASR mis-recognition (e.g., K-V cash -> KV cache). Some timestamps approximate actual utterance time.

Referenced links/resources to attach (to be included in follow-up email):
- sample-audit-schema.json (will be shared)
- SOC2_TypeII_summary.pdf (will be attached)
- POC-runbook-export-CMEK.md (link placeholder)

End of transcript.

title: SE scenario simulator: competitive demo practice
preview:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-12-03
Start: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~90 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Kim (AE), Priya Natarajan (SE), Marcus Lopez (Product), Allie Park (Field AE), Sam Winters (AE), Diego Ramos (SE - observer)

Summary:
- Internal SE/AE practice session focused on competitive demo scenarios and rebuttals.
- Goal: tighten a 6-8 minute core demo, surface quick responses for security/dedicated questions, and rehearse a live failure/fallback story.
- Emphasis on messaging shifts vs OpenAI/Anthropic and when to push Dedicated or Private.

Topics covered:
- Demo timing and choreography (intro, live, perf slides, pricing talk, next steps)
- Competitive differentiators (cost control levers, private deploy, routing/fallbacks)
- Handling objections about latency vs cost, enterprise compliance (SOC2, SSO, KMS), model ownership vs serving
- Roleplay: customer asks about Bedrock price and model portability
- Technical snippet to show KV-cache and batching benefits (quick script)

Action items (captured above)

Transcript body:
[00:00] Jordan: alright everyone, thanks for joining, uh, 90 minutes today, we want to run two full scenarios and then a short Q&A. priya you ready to lead the demo rehearsals?
[00:14] Priya: yeh, yep i am — i'm gonna do the intro, run the 6 minute core and then leave 20 for the scenario switches. if we hit time we cut the perf deep dive.
[00:28] Allie: Heads-up i'll play the skeptical buyer, okay? i'm gonna ask early about cost and private deployment.
[00:35] Sam: i'll be the procurement person, ask legal and timelines.
[00:42] Marcus: i'll keep an eye on the slide deck and flag any backward phrasing — you used "Redwood Privet" in the last rehearsal so watch that.
[00:53] Priya: oh god — thanks. yeah, i have script v4, timing, switch cues. starting now — intro: "Redwood Inference helps you serve LLMs with predictable latency and cost; same API for hosted, dedicated, and private."
[01:12] Priya: Demo plan, 6 minutes: 1) quick problem statement and impact, 2) live gen with context caching, 3) show tracing/cost dashboard, 4) callouts for private/vpc and SLA.
[01:28] Jordan: callout — when customer interrupts on cost say: "we've got three levers: batching, prefix/KV cache, and quantization-profile routing" — short example please.
[01:40] Priya: yep: "for customer X we reduced per-token cost by 40% with prefix caching + int8 quantization on ded pools" — i won't use real customer names, i'll say anonymized customer.
[01:55] Allie (as customer): hold on, 40 percent sounds great but which model are you using and what's latency at 128 tokens?
[02:05] Priya: good question. we can cite our profile for redwood-3b-q8 which hits median p50 110ms on A10X-ish rigs at 128 tokens, and dedicated pools get more predictable percentiles.
[02:20] Sam: (procurement) what's the SLA look like? uptime and remedy?
[02:27] Jordan: standard dedicated contracts have 99.9% uptime with credits; hosted API is best-effort but we have regional routing and fallbacks. mention enterprise add-ons: extended retention, custom SLAs.
[02:40] Diego: quick note, don't say "best-effort" as a phrase in enterprise pitches. maybe "regionally redundant with automated fallback" is better.
[02:51] Priya: noted.
[03:00] [Demo live start] Priya: okay, i'm starting the live chat demo. context: customer support - summarize recent ticket thread, then generate a suggested response and reranking.
[03:12] [00:03:12] Priya: typing... (audio glitch) ... okay, sent. you can see the streaming tokens appear — we use streaming to keep perceived latency down, and the client updates badge with token-level time.
[03:32] Allie (interrupt): are you doing any pre-warming? cause our production load spikes every 9am-10am pst.
[03:38] Priya: good point. we recommend scheduled cache warmers and autoscale policies on dedicated. also smart routing: route to warmed nodes in the same region and fallback to a lower-cost model if capacity constrained.
[03:51] Jordan: demo note: show the runtime trace in the console now — the token trace, the KV hit ratio, and the batch size.
[04:05] Priya: pulling the trace — okay you can see a KV hit of 62% on that call, which reduces the effective generation cost by nearly half for that user.
[04:18] Allie (customer): sounds promising but Anthropic has a whole managed cluster offering — why not use them?
[04:27] Priya: quick answer — Anthropic gives a managed model experience, sure, but we differentiate on three fronts: 1) transparent unit economics and token-level cost controls, 2) ability to pick dedicated capacity with guaranteed percentiles, and 3) a unified API so you can switch models without rewriting clients.
[04:45] Marcus: i would add an example — "we swapped a customer from a hosted large model to a ded + quantized variant and kept 95% of quality while cutting cost by 60%."
[04:58] Sam (procurement): what about data security — SSO and KMS?
[05:05] Priya: we have SAML/SSO, RBAC, role separation, audit logging, and KMS integrations — for private deployments we support HSM-backed keys. SOC 2 Type II in process (or if we have it say yes).
[05:20] Jordan: interjection — show a short screenshot of the audit logs and the KMS key rotation screen. keep it to one slide.
[05:32] [Switch to failure scenario] Jordan: okay next scenario — simulate the primary model going OOS (out of service) and show routing/fallback. Priya you'll intentionally trigger a model fail?
[05:42] Priya: yes, i'll simulate an endpoint exception and show the automatic fallback to a compatible smaller model and the customer-facing degrade message.
[05:51] [Demo] Priya: injecting a 503 to the runtime... there we go — routing to model redwood-3b-lite, tokens stream with slightly different tone, latency increases by ~40ms but it's still within SLA window.
[06:10] Allie (customer): how do you ensure quality doesn't tank on fallback?
[06:16] Priya: two answers: 1) we have model families and curated fallbacks with similar temperature/decoding settings, 2) you can pin prompts and use a quality monitor that raises alerts if metric regresses.
[06:28] Jordan: practice line: "we can configure an A/B fallback where the system tries the primary, if quality dips we automatically route traffic to the fallback and raise a regression ticket." short and tight.
[06:40] [Time check] Jordan: good, that's 8 minutes; stop. pause for feedback.
[06:46] Allie: the intro was fine but the cost convo felt too engineering-y; we need a 20-second soundbite: "We reduce unit cost with three levers so you can scale without surprise bills."
[06:59] Priya: ok i'll add that.
[07:04] Sam: procurement angle — also add a line about procurement-friendly billing: monthly reserved capacity + overage model.
[07:10] Jordan: move on to the competitive roleplay — Allie will press on Bedrock pricing.
[07:18] Allie (customer): i've been quoted cheaper on Bedrock for embeddings, why pick Redwood?
[07:24] Priya: two pieces: 1) Bedrock may list lower unit rates but they hide cross-region egress and you lose unified routing and observability, 2) we help manage model hops to minimize egress and have token-level cost analytics so you really know the true bill.
[07:40] Diego: small nit — avoid talking badly about other vendors; instead say "we provide..." then call out measurable differences: unified observability, SLA-backed ded pools, and built-in quality regression alerts.
[07:52] Marcus: also mention the Optimize toolkit — we give automated quantization and batching suggestions tailored to the workload. that's a differentiator against generic endpoints.
[08:02] Priya: ok adding the Optimize callout.
[08:08] [Roleplay shift - deep technical objection] Sam (procurement): some customers insist on on-prem because of regulatory constraints. how smooth is Redwood Private?
[08:18] Priya: Redwood Private ships with a private control plane and optional on-prem package; it's designed for VPCs and air-gapped installs. typical timeline is 6-10 weeks depending on networking and KMS requirements. we also provide a checklist.
[08:32] Allie: customers will ask about auditability — what evidence do we have to show?
[08:36] Jordan: show them the audit log sample, SOC2 docs, and our deployment runbook. leave a copy of the security packet after the call.
[08:46] [Mid-call coaching] Marcus: priya, you used the phrase "we can do better" twice — try the assertive frame: "what we recommend is..."
[08:54] Priya: noted, will update script.
[09:00] [Benchmarks discussion] Marcus: small point — when quoting latency numbers, be explicit about hardware and token length. say "p50 on A100 80ms at 64 tokens" rather than generic numbers.
[09:12] Priya: agreed — i'll add a slide appendix with p50/p95, model, token length.
[09:20] Jordan: for reps, memorize three quick comparisons vs competitors: cost controls, ded SLA/perf, private deployment story.
[09:30] Allie: ok let's run the second scenario — enterprise procurement deep-dive.
[09:36] [Scenario 2 start] Priya: setup: large finance customer, wants private deploy, wants proof of compliance and pricing prediction.
[09:45] Sam (procurement): can you show a pricing worksheet?
[09:49] Priya: i have a simplified spreadsheet link to share, i'll walk through how to estimate tokens, bucket for peak concurrency, and where reserved capacity pays off.
[10:00] Priya: here's the key line: reserved capacity amortizes base throughput cost — if you have steady high concurrency it's cheaper. for spiky loads we recommend a blended reserved+burst approach.
[10:12] Sam: and what about overage math?
[10:14] Priya: overage is charged at X% above the committed rate; in negotiation we often include a seasonal cushion. (note: exact numbers are redacted in training materials).
[10:26] Allie: legal will ask for data handling specifics — where is data stored and how long retained?
[10:31] Priya: by default hosted is ephemeral but we have retention configs; for Private you control retention and region. we can offer contracts with short retention windows and encryption at rest with customer-managed keys.
[10:42] Diego: mention that we integrate with common KMS providers and have rotation policies.
[10:47] Jordan: good. quick timebox — 20 minutes left for lightning practice of objections.
[10:53] [Lightning objections round - rapid fire lines]
[11:00] Allie: objection - "We want to own the model weights."
[11:04] Priya: answer - "We support private deployments and can assist with model onboarding; but our value is in serving and operational controls so you don't need to build the infra."
[11:12] Sam: objection - "We're worried about vendor lock-in."
[11:15] Priya: answer - "We expose a uniform API and support model portability; rollback or export is part of the dedicated/private offering."
[11:25] Allie: objection - "What if inference cost spikes unexpectedly?"
[11:28] Priya: answer - "We provide token-level dashboards, alerts, and autoscale policies to prevent surprise bills; also a spend cap option for hosted customers."
[11:36] Jordan: good — keep answers <20s each.

title: Cross-region Routing Policy Simulation & KV-cache Recovery Design
preview:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-11-12
Start: 16:00 UTC | Duration: 58 mins
Attendees: Jordan Lee (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Erik Solis (NimbusPay CTO), Maya Chen (NimbusPay SRE Lead), Liam O'Rourke (NimbusPay Platform), Sofia Ruiz (Product)

Auto Summary (auto-generated, may be noisy):
- Discussed cross-region routing policies, capacity knobs, and DR playbook steps.
- Focus on KV cache warmup/rehydration, prefix caching, batching impacts during failover.
- Agreed Redwood will run sim and NimbusPay to share traffic profile; follow-up on VPC/on-prem checklist.

Topics: routing policy simulation, KV cache consistency & recovery, DR playbook steps, quantization tradeoffs for edge replicas, batching and latency SLO impact, VPC peering and private deployments.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Jordan: ok thanks everyone for joining, we wanted to run through the cross-region simulation patterns that we've been validating and, um, map them against NimbusPay's SLOs. are we good on time?
[00:15] Erik: yeah this is a good slot, we got the SRE and the platform folks here, i'll just flag that during black friday windows we see a 6x spike and some very uneven region split because of payment flows.
[00:30] Priya: perfect, and to set expectations this will be a technical deep dive — we will sketch policy routing, show what we simulated for cascading failures, and then deep into KV-cache recovery heuristics. feel free to interrupt.
[00:45] Maya: quick ask — can we also cover the on-prem use-case? we're evaluating a hybrid where token processing remains in our VPC for PCI-sensitive commits.
[00:58] Jordan: yes we'll call that out, the private deployment implications are mostly around egress and certs, plus how the kube sidecar handles KV rehydration.
[01:12] Liam: sidecar? do you mean the Redwood agent in the private deployment or something else?
[01:20] Priya: yeah sorry — Redwood agent/sidecar in the VPC that handles local prefix cache and routes to remote regions if local capacity is exhausted. sometimes transcripts swap words so it's agent/sidecar.
[01:38] Jordan: ok jumping into slide 1, um, high-level policy options: strict-regional (stick to region unless capacity breached), latency-first (route to region with lowest p95), cost-fallback (route to cheaper quantized variant), and staged-failover (canary then full).
[01:55] Erik: our main concern is the payment tokenization path — latency spikes hurt conversions; we want latency-first but also deterministic failures so customers don't get partial tokens.
[02:12] Priya: makes sense. in our sims we created a policy graph: primary region handles 70% steady traffic, backup region 20%, remote cold 10%. then we simulated primary region going to 50% degraded capacity (node OOMs, network flaps) and measured p50/p95/p99 shifts.
[02:35] Maya: did you simulate KV cache warmup times? that's the big risk for latency-first routing because remote region cold start can add 200-800ms while rehydrating.
[02:48] Priya: yes, noted — our KV warmup latencies depend on prefix hit rate; we modelled warmup as parallel prefetch of top N prefixes with adaptive concurrency. in worst case with no prefix cache and large prompt context it was ~700ms extra.
[03:10] Jordan: and quantization interacts here: smaller int8 models reduce compute but can increase token variance and slight quality drift — that matters for tokenization correctness in payments.
[03:28] Erik: we can't accept quality regression on the token step, so quantized fallback is OK for non-critical paths but not for tokenization.
[03:40] Priya: agreed — that's why policy routing can have per-route model profiles: e.g., route A -> float16 primary; route A-tokenization -> pinned full-precision endpoints in regional zone.
[03:58] Liam: how do you enforce those per-route pins in the private deployment? we don't want traffic to leave the VPC for tokenization at all.
[04:10] Jordan: Private deployments allow pinning model variants to local pools; plus we support local-only routes which have a strict deny rule for egress. that does mean capacity planning is required — reserved pools or dedicated capacity.
[04:28] Maya: on capacity — if we reserve capacity, what's the failover behaviour for sudden surges? do we have a burst policy to the cloud-hosted?
[04:42] Priya: yes, you can configure burst_fallback which will route excess requests to hosted Redwood endpoints or to another dedicated pool in a different region. for PCI paths you'd disable burst_fallback to hosted.
[04:57] Jordan: moving to the simulation results — Priya, can you walk the heatmap?
[05:05] Priya: sure. so we ran 3 scenarios: graceful degraded (partial node loss), abrupt outage (complete region down), and cross-region network partition. the important signals were: KV cache miss rate surged 3-8x in abrupt outage; batching increased latency tail because requests got de-batched across regions; and prefix prefetch helped reduce p95 by about 180ms in degraded but only 60ms in full outage.
[05:34] Erik: batching increased tail? can you clarify — we'd thought batching improves throughput and amortizes compute.
[05:42] Priya: yes normally it does, but during failover the request arrival pattern becomes more bursty and heterogeneous; the runtime's continuous batching pools end up with diverse sequence lengths so one long sequence blocks many short ones causing head-of-line blocking — so you see p99 increase.
[06:00] Liam: is there a knob to prioritize short requests in the batch?
[06:05] Priya: we have a batching policy parameter: max_batch_wait_ms and a length-aware queue that can be tuned to prefer short sequences, also a dynamic batch-splitting strategy in the runtime to mitigate H-O-L blocking. but that needs careful tuning per route.
[06:23] Maya: that sounds like a lot of operational overhead. can Redwood recommend presets for payment-heavy workloads?
[06:29] Jordan: yes we have recommended profiles: payment-sso (low latency, small batch, no quantization), high-throughput notifications (larger batch, aggressive quantization), and hybrid. we'll share the preset configs.
[06:43] Priya: back to KV cache recovery — we propose a three-phase DR playbook: 1) detect and mark region degraded, 2) prefetch top-k prefixes into backup region proactively, 3) route traffic gradually with traffic shaping while monitoring semantic correctness metrics.
[07:02] Erik: how do you compute top-k prefixes? we can't expose our raw prompts, so is this done client-side?
[07:12] Priya: two options: you either share anonymized prefix fingerprints (hashes) under an NDA, or run the prefix-extract job inside your VPC with our agent that uploads only the IDs. many enterprises prefer the latter.
[07:26] Sofia: okay that seems acceptable, we'll follow up on an NDA and an agent run.
[07:33] Jordan: good. another practical note — during failover TTLs and KV eviction policies matter; we saw cases where LRU evicted useful entries because burst traffic caused many one-off prefixes, so using an LFU-or-decayed-frequency eviction improved hit-rate.
[07:53] Priya: also consider KV sharding by tenant id or route id; if your tokenization route is heavy, pin its prefixes to a dedicated shard to avoid noisy neighbor evictions.
[08:08] Erik: what's the approximate storage/ram cost for keeping top-k prefixes for a region?
[08:14] Priya: rough numbers: keeping top 100k prefixes per region at average key size ~300B is about 30MB — but embedding KV entries with metadata increases size; plan for 2-3x depending on retention and embeddings.
[08:30] Liam: that's fine. how about prewarming time for those 100k keys?
[08:35] Priya: prefetch concurrency matters — with 64 parallel worker threads prefetching from a remote DB or blobstore we saw 4-8 seconds for 100k keys, but with network limits or cold stores that can go to 30s.
[08:52] Jordan: and for private/on-prem this prefetch is local and much faster assuming you host the prefix snapshot in an NFS or local cache.
[09:03] Maya: switching gears — the DR runbook. can we get a short checklist? we need something we can paste into our incident playbook.
[09:12] Jordan: yes quick checklist: 1) detect via region health probes & p99 anomaly detector, 2) mark region as degraded, 3) enable prefix prefetch to backup, 4) shift small percentage traffic (5-10%) to backup for 2-5 minutes, 5) validate token correctness via canary route, 6) ramp to 50% before full cutover, 7) monitor semantic and conversion metrics, 8) revert if quality drops.
[09:39] Erik: what do you mean by canary route validation for tokens?
[09:44] Priya: run a set of deterministic prompts that exercise tokenization edge cases and assert output matches a golden baseline; we can wire that into your CI and Redwood will surface the daily canary pass rate.
[09:58] Liam: that requires storing golden responses; any security concerns?
[10:03] Priya: the golden can be stored locally in your VPC; Redwood's agent will run the checks and report only pass/fail and hashed diffs. no PII needs to leave.
[10:15] Maya: how does RBAC work for the private agent? we need to restrict who can trigger prefetch/pin actions.
[10:22] Jordan: RBAC ties to your SSO (SAML/Okta) and we expose roles: admin, ops, read-only, and prefetcher. actions are audited in the console and you can require MFA.
[10:34] Erik: let's talk about the network path during failover — how do you prevent traffic blackholing or split-brain when region-A can reach region-B but region-B can't reach region-A?
[10:44] Priya: good question. we model asymmetric partitions: our routing uses last-known-good and health-probe TTLs; if B cannot reach A we avoid bidirectional primaries and default to a single active-active config with egress tagging to prevent duplicate side-effects. we also recommend idempotency keys on tokenization calls to avoid double-processing.
[11:08] Jordan: that idempotency advice is key — for payment flows make sure the API client includes a request-id that the server uses to dedupe.
[11:16] Erik: do you have tooling to simulate asymmetric partitions?
[11:20] Priya: yes our sim engine supports network partitions, packet loss, and node flapping; we can run a tailored sim to match your topology.
[11:29] Jordan: time check — we have about 40 more minutes so let's go deeper into the simulation outputs and then map to an execution plan.
[11:36] Priya: ok next heatmap: per-region capacity curves. we tested three instance sizes and tracked p50/p95/p99 under increasing RPS with and without quantization. results: quantized int8 gave 1.7x throughput improvement but at certain prompt types the non-determinism caused 0.5-1% semantic regressions.
[11:59] Maya: for us 1% degradation on tokenization is unacceptable. but maybe for conversational receipt generation it's fine.
[12:08] Priya: exactly, so per-route model profiles again — route tokenization -> full precision, route receipts -> quantized.
[12:16] Liam: operationally, when a route is promoted to fallback and uses quantized model, is there a way to fall back to float if the semantic monitors detect drift?
[12:26] Priya: yes you can set automated rollback triggers based on semantic metric thresholds — integration with your metrics stack (Datadog/Prom) or Redwood analytics.
[12:36] Jordan: note that automated rollback has a race condition if traffic is already split across variants; our rollout policy supports traffic mirroring to gradient-check before cutover.
[12:47] Erik: we need to know the observability signal set we should ship — what are the minimal metrics?
[12:52] Priya: p50/p95/p99 latency, queue depth, batch size distribution, KV hit rate, prefix prefetch queue length, model confidence score (if available), semantic drift score (similarity to baseline), cost per token.
[13:12] Maya: can we get an example dashboard?
[13:16] Jordan: yes we'll share a dashboard JSON for Datadog and a Grafana starter.
[13:22] Priya: now on to failure-mode KV rehydration sequencing — there's two approaches: eager prefetch (push top-k to backups) and lazy rehydrate (on miss fetch from origin). eager reduces latency but increases overhead; lazy is cheaper but has tail latency issues.
[13:40] Liam: what's the hybrid?
[13:43] Priya: prioritized prefetch — prefetch only request-critical prefixes (tokenization) eagerly and lazily load the rest. prioritize by route tag.
[13:53] Erik: can we tag tokenization prefixes in our agent?
[13:57] Priya: yes the client-side agent can label routes and the prefetcher respects those tags.
[14:03] Jordan: another operational trick — maintain a small local L1 cache for the top 5k prefixes with super-fast eviction and a larger L2; during failover promote L1 to warm.
[14:16] Maya: what about cost implications for multi-region storage of KV dumps?
[14:21] Priya: depends on retention; snapshots to object storage across 3 regions are small but egress costs on restore can add up; we recommend storing compressed, sharded snapshots and using regional caches to avoid cross-region pulls.
[14:36] Erik: switching to security — how do we handle KMS access for private deployments when fetching secret models or keys?
[14:44] Jordan: Redwood supports KMS integrations and customer-managed HSMs; the agent can be configured to request short-lived keys and all accesses are logged. for on-prem you can configure a local KMS proxy.
[14:58] Liam: any PCI-specific caveats?
[15:01] Priya: ensure model access keys never leave the VPC and that the prefetch artifacts don't contain PII. run the agent in restricted IAM role. Redwood can provide a SOC2/PCI checklist.
[15:13] Jordan: we'll attach that checklist to the follow-up thread.
[15:18] Maya: ok can we talk about test harnesses for measuring real p99 under failover — synthetic tests are one thing but real traffic has weird patterns.
[15:28] Priya: we recommend a replay framework: capture production traffic as anonymized traces, replay into a staging contour with capacity constraints and then run the sim. we provide the replay tooling and a corpus sanitizer.
[15:41] Erik: good — that will be part of our POC. timeline wise we want a POC before holiday peak.
[15:48] Jordan: proposed POC plan: week 1 gather traffic profile and set up agent in staging, week 2 run replay and initial sim, week 3 implement prefetch policies and run failover drills, week 4 tune batching and final runbook.
[16:03] Sophia: (inaudible)
[16:08] Priya: last technical note — model kernel selection plays a role: certain kernels have better small-batch perf, others better large-batch. in heterogeneous failover you might select kernel per-device dynamically.
[16:22] Liam: can we pin kernel selection? we don't want surprises.
[16:25] Jordan: yes you can pin preferred kernel families per pool; default is auto.
[16:31] Maya: any gotchas for VPC peering latencies?
[16:35] Priya: peering is fine but watch MTU and TCP windowing — small MTU mismatches can add jitter. test path MTU in advance.
[16:45] Erik: thanks. can you run the tailored sim and show the CSV outputs?
[16:50] Priya: we will — our sim will produce per-route CSV with p50/p95/p99, KV hit rates, prefetch times, and cost delta per million tokens.
[16:59] Jordan: closing — recap and next steps: Redwood runs sim, Nimbus shares traffic, schedule security/VPC checklist. any final Qs?
[17:10] Maya: none from me.
[17:12] Erik: thanks everyone.
[17:15] Jordan: thanks all, we'll follow up with the artifacts.

End of transcript.

- Focus on KV cache warmup/rehydration, prefix caching, batching impacts during failover.
- Agreed Redwood will run sim and NimbusPay to share traffic profile; follow-up on VPC/on-prem checklist.

Topics: routing policy simulation, KV cache consistency & recovery, DR playbook steps, quantization tradeoffs for edge replicas, batching and latency SLO impact, VPC peering and private deployments.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Jordan: ok thanks everyone for joining, we wanted to run through the cross-region simulation patterns that we've been validating and, um, map them against NimbusPay's SLOs. are we good on time?
[00:15] Erik: yeah this is a good slot, we got the SRE and the platform folks here, i'll just flag that during black friday windows we see a 6x spike and some very uneven region split because of payment flows.
[00:30] Priya: perfect, and to set expectations this will be a technical deep dive — we will sketch policy routing, show what we simulated for cascading failures, and then deep into KV-cache recovery heuristics. feel free to interrupt.
[00:45] Maya: quick ask — can we also cover the on-prem use-case? we're evaluating a hybrid where token processing remains in our VPC for PCI-sensitive commits.
[00:58] Jordan: yes we'll call that out, the private deployment implications are mostly around egress and certs, plus how the kube sidecar handles KV rehydration.
[01:12] Liam: sidecar? do you mean the Redwood agent in the private deployment or something else?
[01:20] Priya: yeah sorry — Redwood agent/sidecar in the VPC that handles local prefix cache and routes to remote regions if local capacity is exhausted. sometimes transcripts swap words so it's agent/sidecar.
[01:38] Jordan: ok jumping into slide 1, um, high-level policy options: strict-regional (stick to region unless capacity breached), latency-first (route to region with lowest p95), cost-fallback (route to cheaper quantized variant), and staged-failover (canary then full).
[01:55] Erik: our main concern is the payment tokenization path — latency spikes hurt conversions; we want latency-first but also deterministic failures so customers don't get partial tokens.
[02:12] Priya: makes sense. in our sims we created a policy graph: primary region handles 70% steady traffic, backup region 20%, remote cold 10%. then we simulated primary region going to 50% degraded capacity (node OOMs, network flaps) and measured p50/p95/p99 shifts.
[02:35] Maya: did you simulate KV cache warmup times? that's the big risk for latency-first routing because remote region cold start can add 200-800ms while rehydrating.
[02:48] Priya: yes, noted — our KV warmup latencies depend on prefix hit rate; we modelled warmup as parallel prefetch of top N prefixes with adaptive concurrency. in worst case with no prefix cache and large prompt context it was ~700ms extra.
[03:10] Jordan: and quantization interacts here: smaller int8 models reduce compute but can increase token variance and slight quality drift — that matters for tokenization correctness in payments.
[03:28] Erik: we can't accept quality regression on the token step, so quantized fallback is OK for non-critical paths but not for tokenization.
[03:40] Priya: agreed — that's why policy routing can have per-route model profiles: e.g., route A -> float16 primary; route A-tokenization -> pinned full-precision endpoints in regional zone.
[03:58] Liam: how do you enforce those per-route pins in the private deployment? we don't want traffic to leave the VPC for tokenization at all.
[04:10] Jordan: Private deployments allow pinning model variants to local pools; plus we support local-only routes which have a strict deny rule for egress. that does mean capacity planning is required — reserved pools or dedicated capacity.
[04:28] Maya: on capacity — if we reserve capacity, what's the failover behaviour for sudden surges? do we have a burst policy to the cloud-hosted?
[04:42] Priya: yes, you can configure burst_fallback which will route excess requests to hosted Redwood endpoints or to another dedicated pool in a different region. for PCI paths you'd disable burst_fallback to hosted.
[04:57] Jordan: moving to the simulation results — Priya, can you walk the heatmap?
[05:05] Priya: sure. so we ran 3 scenarios: graceful degraded (partial node loss), abrupt outage (complete region down), and cross-region network partition. the important signals were: KV cache miss rate surged 3-8x in abrupt outage; batching increased latency tail because requests got de-batched across regions; and prefix prefetch helped reduce p95 by about 180ms in degraded but only 60ms in full outage.
[05:34] Erik: batching increased tail? can you clarify — we'd thought batching improves throughput and amortizes compute.
[05:42] Priya: yes normally it does, but during failover the request arrival pattern becomes more bursty and heterogeneous; the runtime's continuous batching pools end up with diverse sequence lengths so one long sequence blocks many short ones causing head-of-line blocking — so you see p99 increase.
[06:00] Liam: is there a knob to prioritize short requests in the batch?
[06:05] Priya: we have a batching policy parameter: max_batch_wait_ms and a length-aware queue that can be tuned to prefer short sequences, also a dynamic batch-splitting strategy in the runtime to mitigate H-O-L blocking. but that needs careful tuning per route.
[06:23] Maya: that sounds like a lot of operational overhead. can Redwood recommend presets for payment-heavy workloads?
[06:29] Jordan: yes we have recommended profiles: payment-sso (low latency, small batch, no quantization), high-throughput notifications (larger batch, aggressive quantization), and hybrid. we'll share the preset configs.
[06:43] Priya: back to KV cache recovery — we propose a three-phase DR playbook: 1) detect and mark region degraded, 2) prefetch top-k prefixes into backup region proactively, 3) route traffic gradually with traffic shaping while monitoring semantic correctness metrics.
[07:02] Erik: how do you compute top-k prefixes? we can't expose our raw prompts, so is this done client-side?
[07:12] Priya: two options: you either share anonymized prefix fingerprints (hashes) under an NDA, or run the prefix-extract job inside your VPC with our agent that uploads only the IDs. many enterprises prefer the latter.
[07:26] Sofia: okay that seems acceptable, we'll follow up on an NDA and an agent run.
[07:33] Jordan: good. another practical note — during failover TTLs and KV eviction policies matter; we saw cases where LRU evicted useful entries because burst traffic caused many one-off prefixes, so using an LFU-or-decayed-frequency eviction improved hit-rate.
[07:53] Priya: also consider KV sharding by tenant id or route id; if your tokenization route is heavy, pin its prefixes to a dedicated shard to avoid noisy neighbor evictions.
[08:08] Erik: what's the approximate storage/ram cost for keeping top-k prefixes for a region?
[08:14] Priya: rough numbers: keeping top 100k prefixes per region at average key size ~300B is about 30MB — but embedding KV entries with metadata increases size; plan for 2-3x depending on retention and embeddings.
[08:30] Liam: that's fine. how about prewarming time for those 100k keys?
[08:35] Priya: prefetch concurrency matters — with 64 parallel worker threads prefetching from a remote DB or blobstore we saw 4-8 seconds for 100k keys, but with network limits or cold stores that can go to 30s.
[08:52] Jordan: and for private/on-prem this prefetch is local and much faster assuming you host the prefix snapshot in an NFS or local cache.
[09:03] Maya: switching gears — the DR runbook. can we get a short checklist? we need something we can paste into our incident playbook.
[09:12] Jordan: yes quick checklist: 1) detect via region health probes & p99 anomaly detector, 2) mark region as degraded, 3) enable prefix prefetch to backup, 4) shift small percentage traffic (5-10%) to backup for 2-5 minutes, 5) validate token correctness via canary route, 6) ramp to 50% before full cutover, 7) monitor semantic and conversion metrics, 8) revert if quality drops.
[09:39] Erik: what do you mean by canary route validation for tokens?
[09:44] Priya: run a set of deterministic prompts that exercise tokenization edge cases and assert output matches a golden baseline; we can wire that into your CI and Redwood will surface the daily canary pass rate.
[09:58] Liam: that requires storing golden responses; any security concerns?
[10:03] Priya: the golden can be stored locally in your VPC; Redwood's agent will run the checks and report only pass/fail and hashed diffs. no PII needs to leave.
[10:15] Maya: how does RBAC work for the private agent? we need to restrict who can trigger prefetch/pin actions.
[10:22] Jordan: RBAC ties to your SSO (SAML/Okta) and we expose roles: admin, ops, read-only, and prefetcher. actions are audited in the console and you can require MFA.
[10:34] Erik: let's talk about the network path during failover — how do you prevent traffic blackholing or split-brain when region-A can reach region-B but region-B can't reach region-A?
[10:44] Priya: good question. we model asymmetric partitions: our routing uses last-known-good and health-probe TTLs; if B cannot reach A we avoid bidirectional primaries and default to a single active-active config with egress tagging to prevent duplicate side-effects. we also recommend idempotency keys on tokenization calls to avoid double-processing.
[11:08] Jordan: that idempotency advice is key — for payment flows make sure the API client includes a request-id that the server uses to dedupe.
[11:16] Erik: do you have tooling to simulate asymmetric partitions?
[11:20] Priya: yes our sim engine supports network partitions, packet loss, and node flapping; we can run a tailored sim to match your topology.
[11:29] Jordan: time check — we have about 40 more minutes so let's go deeper into the simulation outputs and then map to an execution plan.
[11:36] Priya: ok next heatmap: per-region capacity curves. we tested three instance sizes and tracked p50/p95/p99 under increasing RPS with and without quantization. results: quantized int8 gave 1.7x throughput improvement but at certain prompt types the non-determinism caused 0.5-1% semantic regressions.
[11:59] Maya: for us 1% degradation on tokenization is unacceptable. but maybe for conversational receipt generation it's fine.
[12:08] Priya: exactly, so per-route model profiles again — route tokenization -> full precision, route receipts -> quantized.
[12:16] Liam: operationally, when a route is promoted to fallback and uses quantized model, is there a way to fall back to float if the semantic monitors detect drift?
[12:26] Priya: yes you can set automated rollback triggers based on semantic metric thresholds — integration with your metrics stack (Datadog/Prom) or Redwood analytics.
[12:36] Jordan: note that automated rollback has a race condition if traffic is already split across variants; our rollout policy supports traffic mirroring to gradient-check before cutover.
[12:47] Erik: we need to know the observability signal set we should ship — what are the minimal metrics?
[12:52] Priya: p50/p95/p99 latency, queue depth, batch size distribution, KV hit rate, prefix prefetch queue length, model confidence score (if available), semantic drift score (similarity to baseline), cost per token.
[13:12] Maya: can we get an example dashboard?
[13:16] Jordan: yes we'll share a dashboard JSON for Datadog and a Grafana starter.
[13:22] Priya: now on to failure-mode KV rehydration sequencing — there's two approaches: eager prefetch (push top-k to backups) and lazy rehydrate (on miss fetch from origin). eager reduces latency but increases overhead; lazy is cheaper but has tail latency issues.
[13:40] Liam: what's the hybrid?
[13:43] Priya: prioritized prefetch — prefetch only request-critical prefixes (tokenization) eagerly and lazily load the rest. prioritize by route tag.
[13:53] Erik: can we tag tokenization prefixes in our agent?
[13:57] Priya: yes the client-side agent can label routes and the prefetcher respects those tags.
[14:03] Jordan: another operational trick — maintain a small local L1 cache for the top 5k prefixes with super-fast eviction and a larger L2; during failover promote L1 to warm.
[14:16] Maya: what about cost implications for multi-region storage of KV dumps?
[14:21] Priya: depends on retention; snapshots to object storage across 3 regions are small but egress costs on restore can add up; we recommend storing compressed, sharded snapshots and using regional caches to avoid cross-region pulls.
[14:36] Erik: switching to security — how do we handle KMS access for private deployments when fetching secret models or keys?
[14:44] Jordan: Redwood supports KMS integrations and customer-managed HSMs; the agent can be configured to request short-lived keys and all accesses are logged. for on-prem you can configure a local KMS proxy.
[14:58] Liam: any PCI-specific caveats?
[15:01] Priya: ensure model access keys never leave the VPC and that the prefetch artifacts don't contain PII. run the agent in restricted IAM role. Redwood can provide a SOC2/PCI checklist.
[15:13] Jordan: we'll attach that checklist to the follow-up thread.
[15:18] Maya: ok can we talk about test harnesses for measuring real p99 under failover — synthetic tests are one thing but real traffic has weird patterns.
[15:28] Priya: we recommend a replay framework: capture production traffic as anonymized traces, replay into a staging contour with capacity constraints and then run the sim. we provide the replay tooling and a corpus sanitizer.
[15:41] Erik: good — that will be part of our POC. timeline wise we want a POC before holiday peak.
[15:48] Jordan: proposed POC plan: week 1 gather traffic profile and set up agent in staging, week 2 run replay and initial sim, week 3 implement prefetch policies and run failover drills, week 4 tune batching and final runbook.
[16:03] Sophia: (inaudible)
[16:08] Priya: last technical note — model kernel selection plays a role: certain kernels have better small-batch perf, others better large-batch. in heterogeneous failover you might select kernel per-device dynamically.
[16:22] Liam: can we pin kernel selection? we don't want surprises.
[16:25] Jordan: yes you can pin preferred kernel families per pool; default is auto.
[16:31] Maya: any gotchas for VPC peering latencies?
[16:35] Priya: peering is fine but watch MTU and TCP windowing — small MTU mismatches can add jitter. test path MTU in advance.
[16:45] Erik: thanks. can you run the tailored sim and show the CSV outputs?
[16:50] Priya: we will — our sim will produce per-route CSV with p50/p95/p99, KV hit rates, prefetch times, and cost delta per million tokens.
[16:59] Jordan: closing — recap and next steps: Redwood runs sim, Nimbus shares traffic, schedule security/VPC checklist. any final Qs?
[17:10] Maya: none from me.
[17:12] Erik: thanks everyone.
[17:15] Jordan: thanks all, we'll follow up with the artifacts.

End of transcript.

References and shared artifacts mentioned during call:
- DR runbook draft (link: https://redwood.example.com/s/DR-playbook-nimbuspoc)
- Datadog dashboard JSON (link: https://redwood.example.com/dash/nimbus-pay-dd.json)

title: Per-token sensitivity & hosting options — Synapse Works initial scan
preview:
Meeting Title: Per-token sensitivity & hosting options — Synapse Works initial scan
Date: 2025-10-21
Start: 16:00 UTC | Duration: 52 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Ellis (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Mark Levine (Head of ML, Synapse Works), Aisha Gomez (CTO, Synapse Works), Felix Turner (Infra Lead, Synapse Works)
Links shared: https://redwood.ai/decks/hosted-dedicated-vs-private.pdf, https://redwood.ai/spreadsheets/unit-econ-sample.xlsx

Summary:
Quick intro call to map Synapse's growth plan (search/GPT-style augmentation for user support), get a sense of token shapes and QPS, and explore hosted vs dedicated vs private tradeoffs given a firm monthly budget cap. Focus on per-token economics, batching/caching levers, and compliance constraints. Agreed to exchange sample logs and run a short perf/cost sim.

Topics covered:
- Current workload and where LLMs sit in the stack
- Expected qps, peak vs average, and latency SLOs
- Token distribution and prompt shapes (few-shot vs single-shot)
- Tradeoffs: hosted API vs Redwood Dedicated vs Private (VPC/on-prem)
- Cost levers: batching, KV/prefix caching, quantization suggestions
- Compliance: SOC2, KMS, data residency

Action items (recap at end): Jordan to send pricing and worksheet, Priya to run estimates, Mark/Aisha to share logs and compliance checklist.

Transcript:
[00:00] Jordan Ellis: Hey everybody, thanks for making time — I'm Jordan, AE on the inference team at Redwood, and with me is Priya our solutions engineer. How's everyone doing?
[00:06] Mark Levine: Good, thanks, Jordan. Appreciate you fitting this in. We're, uh, trying to figure where to land between just calling an API and, like, reserving capacity.
[00:12] Aisha Gomez: Yep, hi. We're trying to control monthly burn — we have a cap from finance, roughly 80k USD a month for inference to start.
[00:18] Priya Natarajan: Great — and do you have an idea of expected qps and average request size? That really affects per-token math and batching possibilities.
[00:25] Felix Turner: Right now it's pretty bursty. Baseline is 10 rps with spikes to 120 during business hours, lots of short prompts for augmentation and a few long-context job types. Our token mix is noisy — 70% under 150 tokens, 25% 150–1k, 5% over.
[00:40] Jordan Ellis: Helpful. So for per-token economics, we usually break down two things: raw per-token machine/throughput costs and amortized overhead like connectivity and cold-starts. On Hosted you'll pay per-token + a small request fee; Dedicated you pre-pay for capacity so unit cost drops but you commit.
[00:55] Mark Levine: Can you give a ballpark? I know you can't promise exacts but a rough delta between hosted and dedicated.
[01:00] Priya Natarajan: Roughly — depending on model size and quantization — hosted might be X per 1k tokens, Dedicated could be 30–50% lower unit cost if you fill the machine. But the caveat is utilization — if you have large idle periods, Dedicated's per-token could be worse. We'll run their numbers with your token shapes.
[01:20] Aisha Gomez: When you say 'fill the machine' — what utilization do we need?
[01:24] Priya Natarajan: Typically for Dedicated to be attractive you want sustained utilization >40–50% of the reserved GPU pool. There's autoscale/burst but the committed baseline matters for unit math.
[01:34] Felix Turner: Hmm, our burstiness worries me. We could shape traffic via short queuing or a small fronting cache, is that realistic?
[01:40] Priya Natarajan: Yes, caching and batching are big levers. We do prefix/KV cache that can save a lot for repeated prompts, and continuous batching reduces per-token overhead. For your short prompts, batching windows of 20–50ms can give big wins without harming latency much.
[01:53] Mark Levine: Does Redwood support on-prem KMS or customer-managed keys for the private deployment? That's a must for some of our PII workflows.
[01:59] Jordan Ellis: Yes, Private supports customer KMS and HSM integrations; Dedicated also can integrate with your KMS depending on VPC setup. We'll share the security doc.
[02:06] Aisha Gomez: Another question — residency. We need EU-only storage for a subset of data; can Hosted do that?
[02:12] Priya Natarajan: Hosted has region controls and you can pin to EU region, but if you need complete on-prem air-gapped we recommend Private. There's a middle ground with VPC-hosted Dedicated in EU.
[02:23] Mark Levine: Cool. Let's talk numbers — if our token distribution is what Felix described and monthly token volume starts at 300 million tokens, how does that map?
[02:31] Priya Natarajan: Okay, with 300M tokens — small model/quantized path maybe X; bigger model higher. The big thing is latency SLOs — for sub-200ms p50 you need different kernel choices and less batching. If you can tolerate 300–500ms p95 you can batch more and lower cost.
[02:47] Jordan Ellis: Also consider prefilling a hot KV cache for the repetitive system prompts — that can shave 10–30% off token counts effectively by avoiding repeated context.
[02:55] Felix Turner: When you say 'shave off token counts' — do you mean we actually send fewer tokens or you mean runtime caching reduces compute?
[03:01] Priya Natarajan: Both — prefix cache means you don't re-send common system prompt text to the model, and KV cache reduces compute on long chats because the model reuses past key/value states. So it can reduce both upstream token transmission and GPU cycles.
[03:15] Mark Levine: Got it. What's the friction on doing a pilot? We want to validate unit economics with our real prompts.
[03:20] Jordan Ellis: We usually do a 2–4 week pilot: you send sample traffic, Priya's team runs a simulated run againstHosted and Dedicated-ish configurations and produces a unit-cost and latency profile. We'll need sample logs and a clear definition of success (cost target and latency SLO).
[03:33] Aisha Gomez: Our success criteria are: keep p95 < 400ms for common flows and stay under 75k USD monthly. If that fails we won't proceed.
[03:41] Priya Natarajan: Reasonable. We'll model both extremes: low-latency path and cost-optimized path with batching/quantization. For assurances we'll also show fallback policies — e.g. if Dedicated is saturated, route to Hosted with lower latency SLAs.
[03:56] Felix Turner: About quantization — do you have recommendations? Do we need to retrain or is it inference-only?
[04:00] Priya Natarajan: Inference-only quantization works for most model families we recommend — 8-bit and some 4-bit profiles available. We'll suggest per-model profiles in the report. There is a small quality delta we can measure with your eval prompts.
[04:12] Mark Levine: Any hidden costs — like NVLINK licenses or separate telemetry fees?
[04:16] Jordan Ellis: Good question — no NVLINK license pass-through, telemetry/observability tiers are optional add-ons; core metrics/tracing are included but long retention is an add-on. We'll call those out in the worksheet.
[04:27] Aisha Gomez: How often do customers see major swings in unit cost after rollout? I mean, if our traffic pattern changes in month two, can that blow our budget?
[04:34] Priya Natarajan: If you're on Dedicated and utilization drops, your effective unit cost rises; that's why we recommend staged commit sizes and autoscale/burst and routing policies. We'll show a sensitivity curve in the worksheet.
[04:46] Mark Levine: For the pilot we only have the 80k cap—do you have a recommended pilot budget?
[04:51] Jordan Ellis: Typical pilots are 10–25k over 2–4 weeks to get a representative sample. Given your cap, we can structure a 2-week pilot at 12k and limit peak routing or use sample replay to reduce spend while getting good telemetry.
[05:06] Felix Turner: If we do private deployment later, is migration painful?
[05:10] Priya Natarajan: Not usually — our API and configs are consistent across Hosted/Dedicated/Private, so app-side changes are minimal. The big lift is infra prep (networking, KMS, compliance). We'll provide a migration checklist.
[05:22] Aisha Gomez: Any mention of competitors we should be aware of? We talked about MegaCloud AI internally and someone mentioned ScaleServe.
[05:28] Jordan Ellis: Yep — many evaluate general cloud providers but the difference is observability and per-token controls; we'll highlight that in the comparison doc.
[05:36] Mark Levine: Ok, last nit — can you show how batching impacts p95? We need a clear tradeoff table.
[05:41] Priya Natarajan: Absolutely — we'll include a small table: batching windows, expected p95 and per-token delta, and when caching/quantization are effective.
[05:49] Jordan Ellis: Great. To recap: we'll send the unit-econ worksheet and hosted vs dedicated deck, Priya will run simulations against your sample logs, you (Mark/Aisha) will share logs and compliance checklist. We'll aim for a pilot scoping call next week.
[06:01] Mark Levine: Sounds good. Thanks, Jordan and Priya.
[06:04] Aisha Gomez: Thanks, appreciate it.
[06:06] Jordan Ellis: Thanks all — we'll follow up with the materials by tomorrow.
[06:10] (call ends)

Notes on transcription accuracy: Some technical terms may be slightly transcribed (e.g., KV cache as 'K V cash' or similar). Speaker labels are as recorded; short crosstalk occurred around 03:20.
