[03:41] Priya Natarajan: Reasonable. We'll model both extremes: low-latency path and cost-optimized path with batching/quantization. For assurances we'll also show fallback policies — e.g. if Dedicated is saturated, route to Hosted with lower latency SLAs.
[03:56] Felix Turner: About quantization — do you have recommendations? Do we need to retrain or is it inference-only?
[04:00] Priya Natarajan: Inference-only quantization works for most model families we recommend — 8-bit and some 4-bit profiles available. We'll suggest per-model profiles in the report. There is a small quality delta we can measure with your eval prompts.
[04:12] Mark Levine: Any hidden costs — like NVLINK licenses or separate telemetry fees?
[04:16] Jordan Ellis: Good question — no NVLINK license pass-through, telemetry/observability tiers are optional add-ons; core metrics/tracing are included but long retention is an add-on. We'll call those out in the worksheet.
[04:27] Aisha Gomez: How often do customers see major swings in unit cost after rollout? I mean, if our traffic pattern changes in month two, can that blow our budget?
[04:34] Priya Natarajan: If you're on Dedicated and utilization drops, your effective unit cost rises; that's why we recommend staged commit sizes and autoscale/burst and routing policies. We'll show a sensitivity curve in the worksheet.
[04:46] Mark Levine: For the pilot we only have the 80k cap—do you have a recommended pilot budget?
[04:51] Jordan Ellis: Typical pilots are 10–25k over 2–4 weeks to get a representative sample. Given your cap, we can structure a 2-week pilot at 12k and limit peak routing or use sample replay to reduce spend while getting good telemetry.
[05:06] Felix Turner: If we do private deployment later, is migration painful?
[05:10] Priya Natarajan: Not usually — our API and configs are consistent across Hosted/Dedicated/Private, so app-side changes are minimal. The big lift is infra prep (networking, KMS, compliance). We'll provide a migration checklist.
[05:22] Aisha Gomez: Any mention of competitors we should be aware of? We talked about MegaCloud AI internally and someone mentioned ScaleServe.
[05:28] Jordan Ellis: Yep — many evaluate general cloud providers but the difference is observability and per-token controls; we'll highlight that in the comparison doc.
[05:36] Mark Levine: Ok, last nit — can you show how batching impacts p95? We need a clear tradeoff table.
[05:41] Priya Natarajan: Absolutely — we'll include a small table: batching windows, expected p95 and per-token delta, and when caching/quantization are effective.
[05:49] Jordan Ellis: Great. To recap: we'll send the unit-econ worksheet and hosted vs dedicated deck, Priya will run simulations against your sample logs, you (Mark/Aisha) will share logs and compliance checklist. We'll aim for a pilot scoping call next week.
[06:01] Mark Levine: Sounds good. Thanks, Jordan and Priya.
[06:04] Aisha Gomez: Thanks, appreciate it.
[06:06] Jordan Ellis: Thanks all — we'll follow up with the materials by tomorrow.
[06:10] (call ends)

Notes on transcription accuracy: Some technical terms may be slightly transcribed (e.g., KV cache as 'K V cash' or similar). Speaker labels are as recorded; short crosstalk occurred around 03:20.

title: POC weekly: Lo-bit conversion check-in & regression scan
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-10-21
Start: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~56 minutes
Attendees: Alex Kim (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Ben Carter (CSM) - Redwood; Dana Ortiz (ML Lead), Marcus Lee (Infra), Sofia Ruiz (Product), Tom Becker (Data) - Nimbus Health
Links shared: benchmark spreadsheet (drive), regression deck (slides), security FAQ (link)

Summary (auto-gen, may be rough): Quick sync on week-2 lo-bit (int8/fp8) conversion pilot. Reviewed perf deltas, a few regressions on hallucination-sensitive prompts, discussed calibration set and quant params. Agreed next steps to run a longer stability job and share additional failing prompts.

[00:00] Alex: okay hey everyone, thanks for jumping on, we're on for the lo-bit conversion poc weekly, um, objective today is quick results, what changed since last week, any surprises, and next steps. 
[00:28] Dana: great, thanks Alex, hi everyone. we pushed the int8 build into staging last night, ran a few smoke tests, saw throughput improvements but a couple of quality blips on our extraction prompts.
[00:45] Priya: Can you say "extraction prompts" a bit more — are these the structured-medical-record extraction templates?
[00:52] Dana: yeah exactly, like the patient-summary -> medication list parsing. some fields are missing or merged, like "aspirin 81mg" turned into "aspirin81", which breaks downstream parsers.
[01:10] Marcus: from infra we saw p99 token latency drop roughly 22% on average with INT8 with batch size 8, and peak throughput up ~1.8x, but we did notice occasional 500ms spikes at sequence lengths > 1024 when cache warmup happens.
[01:29] Priya: that spike sounds like KV cache population effects. Did you run with prefix caching enabled?
[01:35] Marcus: yes, prefix cache was on, but we had the per-route cache TTL low and some cache thrashing when many sessions hit similar prefixes.
[01:48] Priya: okay makes sense. For the quality issues: did you run symmetric vs asymmetric quantization, and what about group-wise quant?
[01:56] Dana: we used the default conversion script that Alex sent — I think it was symmetric per-tensor? maybe per-channel for some layers, not sure. the sheet says "autofit" which might be Redwood's auto heuristics.
[02:14] Alex: yeah the conversion used our auto-mode: per-channel for weights where applicable, but activation quant was symmetric with a 8-bit clip; it may not be ideal for those NER-like extraction tokens. we also tried an FP8 build in parallel and the FP8 preserved a lot more of the tokenization boundaries, less merging.
[02:36] Sofia: interesting. so FP8 looked better for QA/extraction, but INT8 showed better throughput — is that the quick TL;DR?
[02:44] Priya: short answer yes. FP8 sits in a sweet spot for quality on those sensitive prompts but its perf gains are smaller, depends on kernel support on the hardware. our INF card supports FP8 fast paths for a subset of ops so the delta was about +1.15x on throughput, but INT8 gave +1.8x.
[03:04] Dana: ok, can you quantify the quality drop — what metrics are we looking at? we have accuracy and a custom entity-F1.
[03:11] Priya: we ran the 2.5k prompt validation set: for INT8 we saw entity-F1 drop from 0.92 to 0.86 on average, with a tail of prompts down to 0.75. FP8 was 0.91 average. perplexity doesn't capture the issue here because it's a tokenization boundary error.
[03:35] Tom: we also instrumented the pipeline and saw that the string post-processing regexes were tripped by missing spaces, so that's a brittle point.
[03:43] Ben: is there a quick remediation for those cases? maybe post-quantization token repair heuristics?
[03:50] Priya: a few options: 1) recalibration with a representative calibration set that includes those extraction examples, 2) per-layer higher precision (mixed-bit), 3) FP8 on sensitive routes only, or 4) software fallback for those prompt patterns.
[04:08] Dana: can we do mixed-bit on only the final attention layers?
[04:13] Priya: yes, we can selectively keep some layers in fp16 while quantizing others to int8. that often recovers most of the quality with modest perf loss. we saw in the sandbox that keeping the last two transformer blocks at fp16 recovered entity-F1 to 0.90 while throughput settled at ~1.4x.
[04:33] Marcus: operationally, switching routing to FP8 for a subset of model variants — how heavy is that? can we do that retargeting with a rollout?
[04:40] Alex: yes, Redwood routing can map by policy: e.g., prompt route or customer feature flag to FP8 variant. you can do canary rollouts and automatic fallback to fp16/int8 if latency or error thresholds trigger. I'll attach an example policy.
[04:56] Dana: cool. a question on calibration: how big should the calibration corpus be? we used 2k examples but maybe that's too small or too biased.
[05:05] Priya: recommended is 8k-10k diverse samples for our models for stable clipping statistics, especially for per-channel quant. We'll re-run with the 10k set and include those rare extraction edge-cases.
[05:20] Tom: I can assemble a 10k set from production anonymized records, but need to know format and redaction guidance.
[05:26] Alex: we have a redaction checklist, I'll forward. Ben, can you also share the drive folder link?
[05:31] Ben: yep I'll put the link in the notes. (link pasted)
[05:40] [06:00] Dana: about the hallucination regressions — we noticed when forced decoding of structured fields the model would sometimes invent measurements. Did you see that with FP8?
[06:10] Priya: less with FP8, more with INT8. I think some activation clipping can nudge logits and change the argmax on boundary cases. This shows up as hallucinated numeric values in the extraction outputs.
[06:23] Sofia: is this a function of tokenizer mismatch or quant noise?
[06:27] Priya: mostly quant-induced perturbation. tokenizer mismatch would be more consistent across builds.
[06:33] Dana: ok, we care more about correctness than absolute latency; our SLO is request p95 < 350ms for end-to-end but accuracy is the highest priority.
[06:44] Alex: noted — we can propose a hybrid policy: critical routes map to FP8 or mixed-bit while non-critical analytics routes can use INT8. Also cost math: INT8 reduced token cost by ~38%, FP8 about 22% in our model.
[07:03] Tom: given the cost delta and our usage patterns, maybe a split routing will be fine. we just need a way to automatically shift traffic if quality drops.
[07:10] Priya: Redwood supports automatic rollback on quality regression if you wire up the eval hooks and quality metrics. we can hook in your entity-F1 and set a threshold. If F1 dips below defined value for N minutes we can failover.
[07:25] Marcus: on infra side we also want to know memory footprint differences, because INT8 reduces GPU memory allowing larger batch sizes; FP8 had slightly higher scratch memory due to cast ops.
[07:36] Priya: correct. INT8 reduced peak GPU memory by ~26% enabling batch 12 in our tests, FP8 gave ~12% reduction.
[07:45] Dana: we had one crash during the INT8 run, stacktrace in the sheet points to a kernel fallback in the runtime, something like a non-contiguous tensor path.
[07:56] Priya: hmm that looks like a known edge-case we logged as issue RF-2311 — happens when group-quant size mismatches certain fused ops. we can patch the converter to avoid that path; I'll include the patch in the repro branch.
[08:12] Alex: good, that should be low effort. Dana, can you share the hardware SKU and driver versions? we noted some discrepancies between driver 520.x and 525.x with our kernels.
[08:22] Dana: sure, staging uses ND-accelerator-1 with driver 520.56. Marcus is checking prod driver versions.
[08:29] Marcus: prod is on 525.10, we plan a driver upgrade but want stability first.
[08:33] Priya: driver changes can affect FP8 fast-paths; that might explain a lot of perf variance.
[08:41] Dana: ok. other asks: we want to see more detailed per-layer delta heatmaps, not just aggregate F1.
[08:49] Priya: we have per-layer embeddings drift and token-change matrices; I'll export a heatmap and put in the sheet. you'll see which layers show the biggest KL divergence post-quant.
[08:59] Tom: perfect. also we want to test a stress-run: 48 hour job on canary traffic with 1% real traffic mixed in, to catch stability issues.
[09:08] Marcus: we can spin that up in staging. expect around 150k requests/day at 1% traffic; we'll record errors and latency histograms.
[09:17] Alex: great. timeline wise: Priya re-run calibration by Friday (10/24), Dana provides failing prompt sets by Thursday (10/23), then 48h stability job starts Monday the 10/27 and we reconvene Tuesday the 10/28 for results.
[09:33] Sofia: quick question on monitoring hooks: can we emit entity-level F1 to your observability or do we push metrics to our Prometheus?
[09:40] Ben: both are possible. easiest is push to your Prom and we ingest via remote-write to Redwood console, or we can poll your eval endpoint.
[09:49] Tom: we prefer Prometheus remote-write, we'll open a temporary endpoint.
[09:54] Priya: ok we'll add the hook instructions to the notes.
[10:02] Dana: one last thing — HIPAA implications. any concerns with storing calibration or logs?
[10:08] Alex: Redwood Private is available for HIPAA workloads and we have KMS integrations and audit logs. For now you can use redaction and ephemeral artifacts in staging. we'll also share our security FAQ.
[10:19] Dana: thanks.
[10:22] Alex: quick recap: re-run calibration with 10k, share per-layer heatmaps, assemble failing prompts, start 48h stability run next week, and we'll demo routing/fallback policy. I'll put the policy example and the patch link in the notes.
[10:34] Ben: I'll also attach the benchmark sheet and raw logs.
[10:37] Sofia: thanks team, this was useful.
[10:40] Alex: thanks everyone, talk soon.

Auto topics: quantization, int8, fp8, calibration, KV cache, density-of-errors, throughput, p99 latency, rollout policies, security/HIPAA

Action items (as listed above): Priya to re-run calibration, Dana to supply failing prompts, Alex to send fallback policy, Tom to confirm KV cache flags, start 48h stability run on 2025-10-27.

End of transcript.

title: Throughput calibration + KV cache workshop - Constellation AI
preview:
Meeting header
Date/Time: 2025-09-03 15:00 PDT
Duration: 1:15
Attendees: Alex Kim (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Diego Flores (Redwood SRE advocate), Jordan Lee (Constellation CTO), Maya Rivera (ML Eng), Samir Patel (Platform), Lina Ho (Product)

Summary (auto): quick calibration workshop to align on apples-to... sorry, alignment methodology for throughput and latency benchmarks across on-prem and hosted modes. Focus: Kv cache warm vs cold behavior, batching tradeoffs, quantization reproducibility, and how to make an "apples in different orchards" comparison that the infra team will trust.

Links shared: deck: https://redwood.link/throughput-calibration-deck , benchmark harness repo: https://git.redwood.ai/benchmarks/throughput-harness , example spreadsheet: https://sheets.redwood.ai/constellation-benchmarks

--- Transcript body ---
[00:00:05] Alex: hey everyone, thanks for squeezing this in. quick agenda — align on the benchmark matrix, go over the prompt set design, then run through load patterns and what warm vs cold KV cache measurements mean for Constellation's workloads.
[00:00:22] Jordan: sounds good. we — we mainly care about tail latency p95/p99 during steady state and under burst, and costs as tokens/sec. also want to verify that on-prem numbers match what you show in the hosted docs.
[00:00:40] Priya: great. one quick clarification, when you say on-prem do you mean fully air-gapped hardware or a VPC-connected colo?
[00:00:48] Samir: currently it'll be VPC peered colo cluster with private connections, but some teams will want an air-gapped option later. for this run, VPC peering is fine.
[00:01:00] Diego: okay, that makes the networking part simpler — we can use the same tcp windowing assumptions, lower jitter.
[00:01:12] Lina: also, heads up, our product team will use streaming responses for the UI so we care about first-token latency as well as total token latency.
[00:01:25] Priya: perfect, we'll measure first-byte and token throughput.

[00:02:00] Jordan: question on prompt sets — we have slightly different prompt lengths, some 32 tokens, some 512. do you recommend separating by length?
[00:02:15] Alex: yes separate buckets: short (<64), mid (64-256), long (>256). that lets us see where batching helps vs where kv cache dominates.
[00:02:30] Priya: and also importantly, warm vs cold: if you're doing a chat workload where the KV cache is warm (i.e., previous conversation state cached), latency and throughput change dramatically. so benchmarks must include both cold-start (empty cache) and warm-start (preloaded KV state).
[00:02:50] Maya: when you say preloaded, how deterministic is that? like, do we replay the same prefix to warm the cache?
[00:03:00] Priya: good call — the harness supports prefix-replay. we recommend replaying the last 4 turns or up to X tokens until the KV cache is populated, then measure. note: different tokenization across models can change the effective prefix length.
[00:03:20] Speaker 1: hmm, tokenizer weirdness is a godsend for unexpected results.
[00:03:30] Jordan: ha. okay. what's Redwood's approach to getting apples-to-apples across quantized and full-precision runs?
[00:03:40] Alex: two parts — first, fix the random seed and model version, second, define an exact pipeline: same tokenizer, same stopping criteria, same sampling params (topk, temp, etc). for quantization we run an evaluation set to measure quality delta (exact-match or embedding similarity) and only compare latency/cost on models that meet your quality floor.
[00:04:05] Priya: and we capture logits-level divergence metrics as well as perceptual evals (prompt-specific checks). sometimes int8 has tiny noun swaps — we flag those.
[00:04:20] Maya: ok that makes sense. how do you handle batch sizes? our UI uses concurrency of N=12 per instance typically.
[00:04:30] Diego: for throughput tests we vary concurrent requests and per-request batch size. Redwood's runtime has both request-level batching and internal micro-batching — the harness exposes both knobs. we also track tail latency per model variant.
[00:04:48] Jordan: do you instrument KV cache hit rate? we want to correlate hit rate with cost savings.
[00:04:55] Priya: yes we emit hit/miss counts, cache TTLs, and token-level hit ratios in the telemetry. we can export to your prometheus or push to the console.
[00:05:12] Samir: on the VPC side, what network egress do you expect? we have limited peering bandwidth during business hours.
[00:05:22] Alex: we recommend running loadgen from within the same VPC where possible; for peered testing we'll provide a config that throttles to realistic egress caps and simulates cross-AZ latency.
[00:05:35] Lina: what's the plan for warm-up before measuring?
[00:05:40] Priya: default warm-up: 10 minutes at target QPS or until key metrics stabilize (p95 change <5% over two windows). for cache warm benchmarks, we do a targeted prefix load then a short warm ramp.
[00:05:58] Jordan: ok. can we talk about quantization options? int8 vs int4 vs vanillla fp16?
[00:06:10] Alex: fp16 is the simplest and preserves quality. int8 gives the best speed and memory gains on supported kernels, but you need to validate quality. int4 is aggressive — good for batched throughput on high concurrency workloads but has highest risk of hallucinations on some prompts.
[00:06:30] Maya: have you done any specific tests where int8 changed downstream QA metrics?
[00:06:36] Priya: yes — for a summarization workload we saw a 2-3% ROUGE drop int8 vs fp16; for many generative tasks it's smaller. the harness includes a regression test suite you can plug in.
[00:06:52] Speaker 2: is that the bench regression harness linked earlier?
[00:06:56] Alex: yep, that's the one.

title: Voridian Health - Data Privacy & Encryption QnA
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-07-09
Start: 15:30 UTC
Duration: ~60 minutes
Attendees: Alex Chen (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Elliot Brooks (Redwood SE), Marta Lopez (Voridian CISO), Daniel O'Rourke (Voridian Head of Platform), Sanjay Patel (Voridian Legal), Leah Kim (Voridian DevOps)

Summary: quick security and privacy Q&A focused on data handling (prompt retention, no-training guarantees), encryption/KMS, SOC2/ISO posture, RBAC/SSO, audit logs, and data residency. Customer wanted explicit wording for contract and a plan for an on-prem pilot.

Topics: SOC 2 Type II, \"no training on customer data\" confirmation, KMS/HSM options, encryption at rest/in transit, audit log retention, SSO/SAML, RBAC, data residency (EU/US), prompt/content retention policy, PII/PHI handling, on-prem pilot scope.

Transcript:
[00:00] Alex: okay good afternoon everyone, thanks for joining. I think we have an hour blocked. um quick intro — I'm Alex Chen, AE at Redwood Inference. With me are Priya and Elliot from our security and solutions team.
[00:12] Marta: hi all, Marta here, CISO at Voridian. we've got some specific questions on how you treat prompts — like what stays on your side, where it gets stored, and importantly, whether you ever use customer content to train models.
[00:22] Alex: yeah totally, that's like the core question. for hosted customers we have a clear contract clause: we do not train models on customer data unless a customer opts into a training program and signs a separate agreement. so default is no training, no fine-tuning.
[00:35] Daniel: can you say more about that, are there logs or monitoring data that could leak back into model updates?
[00:42] Priya: good question. we separate telemetry and training pipelines. telemetry is used for platform health and we aggregate/metric-ize it. there is no automatic pipeline that feeds production prompts into model training. that said, in the past transcriptions of names sometimes get munged — redwood internal notes — sorry — um transcription artifacts aside, there is an explicit isolation.
[01:05] Sanjay: legally we need a written confirmation: no training on customer data and no retention of prompts beyond X unless we opt in. what does X equal?
[01:13] Priya: by default for hosted API all prompt/content payloads are retained for 30 days for debugging and abuse detection. after 30 days we roll off PII unless there's an active incident. if you purchase Redwood Dedicated or Private deployment, retention can be configured to zero or local retention only.
[01:30] Marta: 30 days is longer than we'd like, because some of the prompts contain PHI. can you support a 0-day retention window for hosted?
[01:38] Elliot: short answer: hosted can't be zero-days today for all telemetry — the way our abuse/quality pipelines work we keep a short buffer. but we can do Dedicated with a contractual retention of zero, or Private where data never leaves your VPC and logs are under your control; we also offer a SOC2 scoping for that.
[01:56] Leah: for the on-prem option, do you have an operator package? we run k8s but need a hardened deploy with FIPS libs.
[02:04] Priya: yes we have an on-prem package — it's not a canned one-click but it's a deployable private control plane. we support FIPS and can integrate with your HSM. that brings up KMS integration — you can point to your KMS (AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, on-prem HSM) and we support envelope encryption.
[02:22] Marta: can you walk through the KMS envelope flow quickly? like where are keys stored, who has access?
[02:29] Priya: sure — high level: model weights are encrypted at rest using a data encryption key (DEK) that is locally stored in the cluster and encrypted with a customer-managed master key (CMK) in the customer KMS. when a model instance spins up we retrieve a wrapped key via KMS decrypt call, then we use it in memory for the runtime. the CMK never leaves your KMS, Redwood does not retain unwrapped keys. in hosted we can manage CMKs or you can supply them via a cross-account key.
[02:56] Marta: do you support HSM-backed keys for FIPS compliance?
[02:59] Priya: yes, HSM-backed CMKs are supported for both Dedicated and Private. we have customers using AWS CloudHSM + AWS KMS with custom key policies.
[03:10] Daniel: audit logs — we need per-token and per-call logs for a year, preferably immutable. can Redwood provide that?
[03:18] Elliot: for Hosted we provide request-level logs and metrics; retention is configurable as an add-on. for Dedicated and Private you can ship logs to your own SIEM or S3 with retention and WORM policies. immutability/WORM can be done on your storage.
[03:33] Sanjay: and what about RBAC and SSO? we need SAML 2.0, SCIM provisioning, and least-privilege roles.
[03:41] Priya: we do SAML SSO and SCIM for provisioning users and groups. our RBAC model supports scoped roles (admin, developer, auditor), and you can configure route-level permissions — for example restrict embedding creation or model deployment to a subset of users.
[03:58] Marta: can you prove you don't surface PII back to other tenants? like if a prompt has SSN will it show up in logs or other customers?
[04:07] Alex: we have masking and detection pipelines for high-risk data elements (SSNs, credit cards, etc.) in transit; they are redacted in logs. also multi-tenant separation is enforced at the routing layer so artifacts are isolated. occasional OCR/transcription errors on names are possible in speaker notes but not shared across tenants.
[04:27] Leah: quick question on network egress and data residency. our corp policy requires EU data to stay in the EU. can we pin a region?
[04:34] Elliot: yes, region pinning is supported for Hosted (we have EU regions) and Dedicated/Private can be deployed inside your VPC in the region you choose. for Hosted ensure your policy and org account are scoped to an EU region and we will not move requests out.
[04:50] Marta: does Redwood have SOC 2 Type II and ISO?
[04:54] Alex: we are SOC 2 Type II and have ISO 27001 attestation. we'll share the latest report.
[04:59] Sanjay: please include summary language for contract: explicit no-training clause, 30-day default retention for hosted with opt-out to Dedicated/Private for zero-retention, and KMS/HSM support with CMKs remaining under customer control.
[05:10] Alex: yes happy to provide suggested contractual language. we'll add a redlined example.

[02:22] Marta: can you walk through the KMS envelope flow quickly? like where are keys stored, who has access?
[02:29] Priya: sure — high level: model weights are encrypted at rest using a data encryption key (DEK) that is locally stored in the cluster and encrypted with a customer-managed master key (CMK) in the customer KMS. when a model instance spins up we retrieve a wrapped key via KMS decrypt call, then we use it in memory for the runtime. the CMK never leaves your KMS, Redwood does not retain unwrapped keys. in hosted we can manage CMKs or you can supply them via a cross-account key.
[02:56] Marta: do you support HSM-backed keys for FIPS compliance?
[02:59] Priya: yes, HSM-backed CMKs are supported for both Dedicated and Private. we have customers using AWS CloudHSM + AWS KMS with custom key policies.

[03:33] Sanjay: and what about RBAC and SSO? we need SAML 2.0, SCIM provisioning, and least-privilege roles.
[03:41] Priya: we do SAML SSO and SCIM for provisioning users and groups. our RBAC model supports scoped roles (admin, developer, auditor), and you can configure route-level permissions — for example restrict embedding creation or model deployment to a subset of users.
[03:58] Marta: can you prove you don't surface PII back to other tenants? like if a prompt has SSN will it show up in logs or other customers?

title: Sierra Systems - Budgeting and Routing Discussion
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-04-29
Start: 15:30 UTC
Duration: 1:12
Attendees: Alex Chen (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Marcus Lee (CTO, Sierra), Anita Rodriguez (Dir ML), Samir Patel (VP Eng)

Summary:
- Discovery focused on unit economics: per-token pricing, batching/caching levers, and tradeoffs between Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.
- Customer has a strict monthly token budget and latency SLOs for interactive assistant product.
- Agreed next steps: share pricing model, run hosted vs dedicated benchmarks, provide sample prompts.

Topics covered:
- Current stack & constraints (latency SLO, regs, data residency)
- Expected QPS / token volumes and burst patterns
- Per-token price sensitivity and target unit cost
- Batching, KV/prefix caching, quantization as cost levers
- Deployment mode tradeoffs (hosted vs dedicated vs private VPC)
- Security/compliance checklist (SOC2, KMS, audit logs)

Transcript:
[00:00] Alex: hey everyone, thanks for joining — we got about an hour fifteen on the calendar, maybe a little long but wanted space. i'm alex, i'm the AE for redwood for this account.  
[00:08] Marcus: hey alex, thanks. i'm marcus, cto at sierra. today it's me, anita, and samir. we want to focus on cost and which deployment makes sense for our usage pattern.
[00:16] Alex: perfect. priya from our infra team will walk through batching and caching levers after i get a quick sense of your constraints. priya?
[00:22] Priya: hi all. quick note: i'll talk about batching, kv cache — sometimes our notes come through as kay vee cache in transcripts — and how those affect per-token. also quantization profiles.
[00:30] Anita: just to set context, our product is an interactive assistant embedded in our app. target p50 latency under 300ms for short prompts, target p95 under 800ms. monthly token budget is ~1.2 billion tokens but can spike to 2.5B in Black Friday–type bursts.
[00:44] Alex: great, that's super helpful. can you share typical prompt lengths? average request tokens?
[00:49] Samir: average prompt 120 tokens in, expecting 160 tokens completion avg, so roughly ~280 tokens per call. peak QPS around 70 sustained, bursts to 250 for a few minutes.
[01:03] Priya: okay, that pattern matters. With a 280-token roundtrip, batching can help a lot for throughput but can increase tail latency. for interactive 300ms p50 goal, we need small batch sizes or dynamic batching.
[01:18] Alex: on pricing, our hosted SKU has a base per-1k-token rate, we typically show examples as $0.60 / 1k toks for our standard open model — that's illustrative — dedicated reservation changes economics to something like $0.40 / 1k toks depending on commit. (note: numbers are illustrative in calls)
[01:36] Marcus: those are helpful benchmarks. do you have an example calc for our 1.2B monthly tokens?
[01:42] Alex: rough math: at $0.60 / 1k, 1.2B tokens => (1,200,000 * $0.60) = $720k / month. with dedicated commit — if you reserve capacity and run with quantization + prefix caching — you can often drop unit cost by 20–40%, so maybe down to ~$432k–576k depending on utilization and bursting model.
[02:06] Anita: that's a big swing. question: how much does prefix/KV caching help for assistant context reuse? our app reuses a lot of system prompts and few-shot examples.
[02:16] Priya: if you have high prefix reuse, effective tokens billed can drop substantially. we do prefix caching at request routing layer — if prefix matches we serve from cache and only bill for novel continuation tokens. in practice, teams with 30–40% prefix reuse see 20–30% reduction in billed tokens. some customers see more, depending on prompt design.
[02:40] Samir: interesting. how do you handle cache invalidation and privacy concerns? our compliance team is strict.
[02:46] Priya: cache is configurable per-tenant. for Private/VPC can be on-customer-network; for Hosted we encrypt keys and support per-tenant segregation. cache invalidation is TTL-based and you can explicitly purge. also we support KMS for key separation.
[03:02] Marcus: on dedicated vs hosted: do you support autoscaling and burst capacity? we can't overcommit in monthly fee.
[03:10] Alex: yes — Dedicated includes autoscale and burst options. typical model: commit to baseline capacity for predictable throughput and pay an overage rate for bursts. we can model 30–50% burst headroom via burst pools.
[03:22] Anita: can you talk about latency differences? hosted vs dedicated vs private?
[03:27] Priya: dedicated and private generally reduce cold-path variability since you have pinned GPUs and fewer multi-tenant effects. hosted is multi-tenant optimized and can be very low lat for many workloads but for strict p95 targets dedicated often wins because you control scheduling, KV cache locality, and can choose lower-precision kernels. private on-prem can give you the best latency if you're colocated near users but adds ops overhead.
[03:50] Alex: also quantization: 4-bit or int8 runs can reduce inference cost 2x–3x but may affect quality for certain models. we recommend evals against your prompt set.
[03:59] Marcus: quality impact is our main fear — we can't regress on accuracy.
[04:03] Priya: agree. we usually run A/B evals across sample prompts and report delta on generation recall/factuality. often quantization is safe for embeddings and many decoders but needs checking for your few-shot assistant style.
[04:17] Samir: what's the recommended approach if we're risk averse but want cost savings?
[04:20] Alex: hybrid: start hosted for quick time-to-value and run a dedicated pilot for your heavy traffic hours. routing can send low-latency, mission-critical requests to dedicated and everything else to hosted. smart routing reduces your committed footprint while preserving SLOs.
[04:36] Priya: plus automatic fallback — if dedicated is saturated we route to hosted model variant that is cost-compatible. that's part of graceful degradation design.
[04:46] Anita: can we see an example cost model that includes caching and batching adjustments?
[04:50] Alex: absolutely, i'll send a spreadsheet that models: base tokens, effective cache hit rate, batching efficiency factor (tokens per GPU-step), quantization uplift, and reserved capacity discount. we'll ask for your sample prompts to tune the numbers.
[05:03] Marcus: about batching: in interactive flows i worry batching will push our p95 over the threshold. any mitigation?
[05:10] Priya: dynamic batching — prioritize latency SLO requests and batch only background or less latency-sensitive requests. continuous batching can also use small micro-batches to preserve latency. you can pick per-route policies in the console. we also have model variants with shorter context lengths for microcalls.
[05:27] Samir: what are typical batching factors we'd see?
[05:30] Priya: depends on request distribution. with 70 QPS steady and 280 tokens avg, you might get batching factor of 2–6 on GPUs depending on max-seqlen. in very bursty traffic those factors jump. the important bit is effective tokens-per-second and how it maps to GPU throughput.
[05:47] Alex: i want to highlight network egress and embedding costs too — embeddings pricing is separate and can add up if you're re-indexing a lot. is that in scope for you?
[05:55] Marcus: not primary now, but likely later. focus first on interactive assistant calls.
[06:00] Anita: security — do you have SOC2 report and encryption at rest? we need audit logs for inference requests.
[06:07] Alex: yes, we have SOC2 and can provide the report. we offer KMS integration for keys, audit logs per request, and RBAC in console. for Private deployments we support on-prem KMS/HSM.
[06:20] Samir: about data retention — can you configure zero retention for input prompts?
[06:25] Priya: yes, retention policies are configurable. for hosted we can support do-not-log on a per-route basis; for private you can control all retention.
[06:33] Marcus: if we were to pilot, what'd a lightweight pilot look like?
[06:37] Alex: 4-week pilot: week 1 instrument sample prompts and run hosted baseline; week 2 run dedicated small reservation and benchmark; week 3 run caching + quantization experiments; week 4 evaluate results and recommend commit/rollout. priya would run bench tests.
[06:54] Priya: bench will include p50/p95 latency, token throughput, cost per 1k at observed utilization, and quality regressions for quantized models. we need your prompts and a fixture to replay traffic.
[07:08] Anita: timeframe wise, if we share prompts by friday can we get bench results in two weeks?
[07:13] Priya: yes, two weeks is feasible. depends on queue but we'll prioritize.
[07:17] Alex: ok, i'm going to summarize next steps at the end, but wanted to flag a few specifics: your monthly budget target was mentioned as roughly $X (Marcus noted $X in call — redacted in transcript), can you confirm a hard cap?
[07:31] Marcus: we said our preferred cap is $500k/month but can spike to 750k with justification.
[07:36] Alex: good to know. given the earlier back-of-envelope numbers, a dedicated commit could get you inside that range if utilization and caching are good.
[07:44] Samir: any hidden fees? data egress, storage for cache?
[07:48] Alex: common line items: storage for large prefix caches (small relative), egress if data leaves region, and overage fees on dedicated. we'll show a line-item breakdown in the sheet.
[07:58] Anita: how do we model the payback period for a reserved commit? we don't want to overcommit if traffic grows slowly.
[08:03] Priya: our pricing calculator supports multi-month runway and utilization curves. typically customers simulate utilization over 6 months and pick a commit that breaks even within 2–4 months based on forecast. if growth is uncertain, smaller commit + burst pool is safer.
[08:18] Marcus: are there examples of customers who moved from hosted to dedicated to save on unit cost? what's the threshold?
[08:24] Alex: threshold varies but typical pattern is when monthly bill exceeds mid-six figures and utilization is steady. many customers start hosted, run a pilot, then move to mixed routing. we'll share anonymized case study.
[08:36] Samir: ok can we get an example cost calc for our numbers in the sheet?
[08:39] Alex: yes, i'll populate the calc with your 1.2B baseline and the spike profile.
[08:44] Priya: small note: caching assumptions are sensitive to prompt churn. if you change few-shot examples often, cache hit drops. we may recommend moving static system prompts into trimmed tokens or using embeddings+lookup to reduce repetition.
[08:58] Anita: that could help. our devs often include variable few-shot examples. we'll work on prompt hygiene.
[09:03] Marcus: final — are there compliance limitations with using hosted vs private for regulated user data?
[09:09] Alex: hosted can meet many compliance needs with SOC2, encrypted transit+rest, and region controls. but if you have strict residency requirements or very sensitive PHI-like data, Private/VPC or on-prem is recommended.
[09:21] Samir: good. that covers most of our questions.
[09:25] Alex: great. quick recap of next steps then i'll close: (1) Alex will send pricing spreadsheet + example model by 2025-05-02; (2) Priya will schedule bench runs and needs sample prompts by 2025-05-01; (3) Marcus to confirm final token cap and provide prompt fixtures; (4) schedule security review if pilot moves forward.
[09:49] Marcus: sounds good. thanks everyone.
[09:52] Anita: thanks.
[09:53] Alex: thanks — will follow up with calendar invites and the sheet. good talk.

End of transcript.

[01:42] Alex: rough math: at $0.60 / 1k, 1.2B tokens => (1,200,000 * $0.60) = $720k / month. with dedicated commit — if you reserve capacity and run with quantization + prefix caching — you can often drop unit cost by 20–40%, so maybe down to ~$432k–576k depending on utilization and bursting model.
[02:06] Anita: that's a big swing. question: how much does prefix/KV caching help for assistant context reuse? our app reuses a lot of system prompts and few-shot examples.
[02:16] Priya: if you have high prefix reuse, effective tokens billed can drop substantially. we do prefix caching at request routing layer — if prefix matches we serve from cache and only bill for novel continuation tokens. in practice, teams with 30–40% prefix reuse see 20–30% reduction in billed tokens. some customers see more, depending on prompt design.
[02:40] Samir: interesting. how do you handle cache invalidation and privacy concerns? our compliance team is strict.
[02:46] Priya: cache is configurable per-tenant. for Private/VPC can be on-customer-network; for Hosted we encrypt keys and support per-tenant segregation. cache invalidation is TTL-based and you can explicitly purge. also we support KMS for key separation.
[03:02] Marcus: on dedicated vs hosted: do you support autoscaling and burst capacity? we can't overcommit in monthly fee.
[03:10] Alex: yes — Dedicated includes autoscale and burst options. typical model: commit to baseline capacity for predictable throughput and pay an overage rate for bursts. we can model 30–50% burst headroom via burst pools.
[03:22] Anita: can you talk about latency differences? hosted vs dedicated vs private?
[03:27] Priya: dedicated and private generally reduce cold-path variability since you have pinned GPUs and fewer multi-tenant effects. hosted is multi-tenant optimized and can be very low lat for many workloads but for strict p95 targets dedicated often wins because you control scheduling, KV cache locality, and can choose lower-precision kernels. private on-prem can give you the best latency if you're colocated near users but adds ops overhead.
[03:50] Alex: also quantization: 4-bit or int8 runs can reduce inference cost 2x–3x but may affect quality for certain models. we recommend evals against your prompt set.
[03:59] Marcus: quality impact is our main fear — we can't regress on accuracy.
[04:03] Priya: agree. we usually run A/B evals across sample prompts and report delta on generation recall/factuality. often quantization is safe for embeddings and many decoders but needs checking for your few-shot assistant style.
[04:17] Samir: what's the recommended approach if we're risk averse but want cost savings?
[04:20] Alex: hybrid: start hosted for quick time-to-value and run a dedicated pilot for your heavy traffic hours. routing can send low-latency, mission-critical requests to dedicated and everything else to hosted. smart routing reduces your committed footprint while preserving SLOs.
[04:36] Priya: plus automatic fallback — if dedicated is saturated we route to hosted model variant that is cost-compatible. that's part of graceful degradation design.
[04:46] Anita: can we see an example cost model that includes caching and batching adjustments?
[04:50] Alex: absolutely, i'll send a spreadsheet that models: base tokens, effective cache hit rate, batching efficiency factor (tokens per GPU-step), quantization uplift, and reserved capacity discount. we'll ask for your sample prompts to tune the numbers.
[05:03] Marcus: about batching: in interactive flows i worry batching will push our p95 over the threshold. any mitigation?
[05:10] Priya: dynamic batching — prioritize latency SLO requests and batch only background or less latency-sensitive requests. continuous batching can also use small micro-batches to preserve latency. you can pick per-route policies in the console. we also have model variants with shorter context lengths for microcalls.
[05:27] Samir: what are typical batching factors we'd see?
[05:30] Priya: depends on request distribution. with 70 QPS steady and 280 tokens avg, you might get batching factor of 2–6 on GPUs depending on max-seqlen. in very bursty traffic those factors jump. the important bit is effective tokens-per-second and how it maps to GPU throughput.
[05:47] Alex: i want to highlight network egress and embedding costs too — embeddings pricing is separate and can add up if you're re-indexing a lot. is that in scope for you?
[05:55] Marcus: not primary now, but likely later. focus first on interactive assistant calls.
[06:00] Anita: security — do you have SOC2 report and encryption at rest? we need audit logs for inference requests.
[06:07] Alex: yes, we have SOC2 and can provide the report. we offer KMS integration for keys, audit logs per request, and RBAC in console. for Private deployments we support on-prem KMS/HSM.
[06:20] Samir: about data retention — can you configure zero retention for input prompts?
[06:25] Priya: yes, retention policies are configurable. for hosted we can support do-not-log on a per-route basis; for private you can control all retention.
[06:33] Marcus: if we were to pilot, what'd a lightweight pilot look like?
[06:37] Alex: 4-week pilot: week 1 instrument sample prompts and run hosted baseline; week 2 run dedicated small reservation and benchmark; week 3 run caching + quantization experiments; week 4 evaluate results and recommend commit/rollout. priya would run bench tests.
[06:54] Priya: bench will include p50/p95 latency, token throughput, cost per 1k at observed utilization, and quality regressions for quantized models. we need your prompts and a fixture to replay traffic.
[07:08] Anita: timeframe wise, if we share prompts by friday can we get bench results in two weeks?
[07:13] Priya: yes, two weeks is feasible. depends on queue but we'll prioritize.
[07:17] Alex: ok, i'm going to summarize next steps at the end, but wanted to flag a few specifics: your monthly budget target was mentioned as roughly $X (Marcus noted $X in call — redacted in transcript), can you confirm a hard cap?
[07:31] Marcus: we said our preferred cap is $500k/month but can spike to 750k with justification.
[07:36] Alex: good to know. given the earlier back-of-envelope numbers, a dedicated commit could get you inside that range if utilization and caching are good.
[07:44] Samir: any hidden fees? data egress, storage for cache?
[07:48] Alex: common line items: storage for large prefix caches (small relative), egress if data leaves region, and overage fees on dedicated. we'll show a line-item breakdown in the sheet.
[07:58] Anita: how do we model the payback period for a reserved commit? we don't want to overcommit if traffic grows slowly.
[08:03] Priya: our pricing calculator supports multi-month runway and utilization curves. typically customers simulate utilization over 6 months and pick a commit that breaks even within 2–4 months based on forecast. if growth is uncertain, smaller commit + burst pool is safer.
[08:18] Marcus: are there examples of customers who moved from hosted to dedicated to save on unit cost? what's the threshold?
[08:24] Alex: threshold varies but typical pattern is when monthly bill exceeds mid-six figures and utilization is steady. many customers start hosted, run a pilot, then move to mixed routing. we'll share anonymized case study.
[08:36] Samir: ok can we get an example cost calc for our numbers in the sheet?
[08:39] Alex: yes, i'll populate the calc with your 1.2B baseline and the spike profile.
[08:44] Priya: small note: caching assumptions are sensitive to prompt churn. if you change few-shot examples often, cache hit drops. we may recommend moving static system prompts into trimmed tokens or using embeddings+lookup to reduce repetition.
[08:58] Anita: that could help. our devs often include variable few-shot examples. we'll work on prompt hygiene.
[09:03] Marcus: final — are there compliance limitations with using hosted vs private for regulated user data?
[09:09] Alex: hosted can meet many compliance needs with SOC2, encrypted transit+rest, and region controls. but if you have strict residency requirements or very sensitive PHI-like data, Private/VPC or on-prem is recommended.
[09:21] Samir: good. that covers most of our questions.
[09:25] Alex: great. quick recap of next steps then i'll close: (1) Alex will send pricing spreadsheet + example model by 2025-05-02; (2) Priya will schedule bench runs and needs sample prompts by 2025-05-01; (3) Marcus to confirm final token cap and provide prompt fixtures; (4) schedule security review if pilot moves forward.
[09:49] Marcus: sounds good. thanks everyone.
[09:52] Anita: thanks.
[09:53] Alex: thanks — will follow up with calendar invites and the sheet. good talk.

End of transcript.

title: Go/No-Go: Dedicated staged rollouts (beta) + incident review + launch checklist
preview: Internal go/no-go for enabling Dedicated staged rollouts for a limited beta (1–2 design partners). Reviewed early beta failures (429 spike due to canary partition starving effective capacity, gate evidence missing due to telemetry tag mismatch, and metrics cardinality regression). Aligning on ship criteria: feature flag default OFF, validated rollback/abort semantics, stable gate evaluation under telemetry lag, and audit export readiness. Decision: GO for limited beta with guardrails and explicit hold/force-promote controls restricted to admin roles; no broad enablement until SIEM export and small-pool safeguards are verified.

title: Northwind AI - usage discrepancy escalation sync
Incident follow-up: us-east capacity + fallback routing
preview:
Meeting header
- Date: 2025-02-04
- Duration: 38 min
- Attendees (Redwood): Vanessa Ortiz, Priya Natarajan, Connor O'Brien, Maya Srinivasan
- Attendees (Customer): Samir Desai, Nina Howard

Summary (auto)
- Discussed what happened during us-east capacity incident, what Redwood changed manually, and the roadmap for automatic fallback routing.

Topics
1. What failed in us-east
2. Pinned model semantics vs "silent model switch"
3. Auto-fallback (region + model variant) roadmap

Transcript
[00:00] Vanessa Ortiz: thanks for jumping on. we wanted to walk through the us-east blip and answer the big question: did the model change on you
[00:18] Samir Desai: yeah. our contract is pretty explicit. if the model changes, we need to know. we saw 502s and then it got better
[00:33] Priya Natarajan: so the improvement was a manual region shift. we moved your traffic from us-east to us-west for about twenty-ish minutes. same model build
[00:50] Nina Howard: was that automatic?
[00:52] Priya Natarajan: not yet. right now it’s runbook + a manual flag. we’re implementing automatic request fallback in the router

[01:10] Connor O'Brien: there are two types we talk about internally: region fallback and model-variant fallback. for pinned customers like you, we will not do model-variant fallback
[01:28] Samir Desai: what do you mean by "variant"? like quantized?
[01:32] Connor O'Brien: exactly, like int8 variant or a different build. even if it’s compatible, we will block it for pinned. the only allowed is region within your allowed geography

[02:05] Maya Srinivasan: and we’re considering an audit header so you can detect when fallback happened. maybe `x-redwood-fallback` with reason codes
[02:20] Nina Howard: reason codes would be great. we need to correlate to our retries

[03:02] Priya Natarajan: on triggers, the router will fallback when error rate is above a threshold, or latency plus high utilization, or if oncall marks a route as degraded
[03:20] Samir Desai: ok but don’t ever move us to EU or something
[03:23] Priya Natarajan: yep, residency constraints are enforced. EU-only never goes to US, and for you we’ll keep to US regions

[05:10] Vanessa Ortiz: action item for us: send a written note that for pinned builds we only do region fallback and we’ll notify you if we ever propose changing that

[36:40] Samir Desai: cool. send the writeup. also, if you can expose counts in the console later, that’s helpful
[37:05] Maya Srinivasan: noted. thanks everyone

title: Security review - Redwood Inference with Northbridge Health
preview:
Meeting Header
Date: 2024-11-12
Time: 2:00pm ET
Duration: 41 min
Attendees: Ben Carter (Redwood), Priya Natarajan (Redwood), Samantha Yuen (Northbridge Health), Miguel Ortega (Northbridge Health), Rina Shah (Northbridge Health)

Summary (auto)
- Discussed audit logging, SOC 2, and data retention options.
- Customer asked about reducing retention for troubleshooting artifacts.

Transcript
[00:00] Ben Carter: Cool, thanks everyone for joining. Quick overview, Redwood does hosted inference and we also have private deployments.
[00:32] Samantha Yuen: Our main questions are around PHI. Do you store prompts or responses?
[01:05] Priya Natarajan: We don't use customer data to train. For hosted, we have limited retention for debugging and abuse prevention, and access is controlled.
[02:10] Miguel Ortega: Right. Were not necessarily asking for zero, but we want the minimum. Whats the shortest retention you can do? Like 7 days?
[03:02] Ben Carter: Hosted has a fixed window today. We can share the exact number offline. In private deployments, you have more control over logging and retention.
[05:14] Rina Shah: So you do have content stored somewhere for a period?
[05:28] Priya Natarajan: Yes, for troubleshooting in certain scenarios. Its not wide-open access; its restricted.
[08:41] Samantha Yuen: Ok. Were not asking for no logging, but we need a clear statement: retention window and who can access.
[10:05] Ben Carter: Understood. Well send a written response and the SOC 2 report under NDA.

[35:10] Ben Carter: Recap: well send the security FAQ + SOC2, and youll confirm whether a 7-day retention policy works.
[40:55] Samantha Yuen: Sounds good.
